export const meta = {
  name: 'pass4-verify',
  description: 'Pass-4: 2-agent blind verification of 24 findings',
  phases: [{ title: 'Verify', detail: '2 agents per finding' }, { title: 'Triage', detail: 'collect verdicts' }],
}
const ROOT = '/Users/thomasjones/hc-assay'
const FINDINGS = [{"id": "G-001", "severity": "high", "file": "src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py", "line": "230-258", "title": "Post-hoc direction selection (HARKing) for whole-corpus hypotheses locked with predicted_direction=None", "description": "_resolve_direction accepts a confirm-time predicted_direction whenever the locked hypothesis has predicted_direction=None, and nothing forces a WHOLE_CORPUS hypothesis to lock a non-None direction. A study can pre-register a directionless whole-corpus hypothesis, inspect the observed statistic/baseline, then choose 'greater' or 'less' at confirm_whole_corpus time to land in whichever tail is significant \u2014 textbook HARKing that defeats the pre-registration binding (issue #24 the docstring claims to close). Verified in source: when locked_dir is None and predicted_direction is None it raises, but when predicted_direction is supplied it is used as the resolved tail, so the verdict polarity is chosen post-hoc on a hypothesis whose cryptographic lock binds 'no direction'.", "poc": "Lock a WHOLE_CORPUS Hypothesis with predicted_direction=None via lock_hypothesis (proof binds None). After observing obs=95.0 against a 0..99 null, call confirm_whole_corpus(..., predicted_direction='greater', authority=auth) -> 'supported'; had obs been low, pass 'less' instead. Pre-registration verification passes; tail chosen after seeing data.", "suggested_fix": "Require predicted_direction at lock time for WHOLE_CORPUS hypotheses (raise in Hypothesis.__post_init__ when kind is WHOLE_CORPUS and direction is None), or in _resolve_direction reject locked_dir is None for any hypothesis going through require_preregistered (accept a confirm-time direction only for unlocked exploratory hypotheses). The directionless-deferred path must be unreachable for a pre-registered confirmatory test.", "dimension": "methodology"}, {"id": "G-002", "severity": "high", "file": "src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py", "line": 212, "title": "Bootstrap lock_timeout session-GUC leaks into pooled connection used for all later checkpoint writes", "description": "_acquire_migration_lock runs SELECT set_config('lock_timeout', %s, false) \u2014 is_local=false, i.e. SESSION scope (confirmed at source line ~210). The connection comes from `with pool.connection() as conn:` and is returned to the same pool on block exit; psycopg_pool does not reset session state without a reset= callback (none configured). That pool is then used by PostgresSaver for every subsequent checkpoint read/write, so the bootstrap-only lock_timeout (default 30s) silently persists on a long-lived pooled connection. Under lock contention a normal checkpoint write that would otherwise block briefly aborts with 'canceling statement due to lock_timeout' \u2014 durability loss at exactly the interrupt/resume the checkpointer exists to guarantee.", "poc": "With ASSAY_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS=30000, run get_checkpointer() to bootstrap, then re-check-out a connection and assert conn.execute('SHOW lock_timeout').fetchone() != '0'. Two concurrent resumes contending on the same checkpoint row produce a spurious 'canceling statement due to lock_timeout' instead of completing.", "suggested_fix": "Reset the GUC on the same connection before block exit after setup()/advisory-unlock (conn.execute(\"SELECT set_config('lock_timeout','0',false)\") or RESET lock_timeout in finally); or configure ConnectionPool(reset=lambda c: c.execute('RESET ALL')); or run bootstrap on a dedicated one-shot psycopg.connect() that is closed rather than returned to the pool.", "dimension": "persistence-durability"}, {"id": "G-003", "severity": "high", "file": "src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py", "line": 253, "title": "BULK tier has no inner cancellation (asymmetric with HIGH_STAKES) \u2014 a hung backend permanently leaks a pool slot and drives endless retryable backpressure", "description": "Confirmed in source: HIGH_STAKES wraps its stream in `anyio.fail_after(HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT)` (seam.py:438) so a hung subprocess is cancelled inside the worker and the bounded-pool slot is freed. BULK has no equivalent \u2014 it relies only on client_kwargs={'timeout': BULK_TIMEOUT} reaching httpx (seam.py:349) and the outer _with_timeout(..., BULK_TIMEOUT+10) (seam.py:510), which by its own docstring cannot interrupt a thread blocked in a backend call. If the httpx timeout does not fire (wedged stream, proxy holding the socket, ChatOllama not propagating client_kwargs, C-level blocking read) the worker is stuck forever and its _inflight slot never releases. _submit_bounded reports saturation as a RETRYABLE RateLimitError ('pool saturated', seam.py:223) and _run_with_retries treats it as transient backpressure, so once enough BULK workers wedge (up to _POOL_WORKERS=8) every future call livelocks on saturation backpressure that never clears.", "poc": "Monkeypatch _bulk_complete to block with no timeout (threading.Event().wait()). Submit _POOL_WORKERS BULK requests via _with_timeout; each returns FuturesTimeout to the caller but the worker stays alive holding its slot. After 8, every _submit_bounded raises RateLimitError('saturated') indefinitely; slots never recover for the rest of process life.", "suggested_fix": "Give BULK the same in-worker hard bound as HIGH_STAKES (run the generation under anyio.fail_after(BULK_TIMEOUT) via an async ollama client, or a cancellable subprocess). At minimum add a watchdog that surfaces a PermanentReasoningError ('reasoning pool wedged') on saturation that never clears rather than infinite RateLimitError backpressure, and assert ChatOllama honors client_kwargs timeout on the streaming path.", "dimension": "reasoning-seam"}, {"id": "G-004", "severity": "high", "file": "src/assay_engine/_frozen.py", "line": 146, "title": "unfreeze() promises 'stable order for reproducible output' for frozensets but the unorderable-element fallback iterates in non-deterministic order", "description": "The unfreeze() docstring (line 146) states 'frozenset -> sorted list (stable order for reproducible output)' and the inline comment (line ~153) claims a 'stable repr'. Confirmed: the implementation tries sorted(value) and on TypeError falls back to [unfreeze(v) for v in value], iterating the frozenset directly. Frozenset iteration order for str elements varies with PYTHONHASHSEED and for FrozenDict/object elements with id-based hashing varies per run, so output order is NOT reproducible \u2014 contradicting both the docstring and the comment (the F-032 class). unfreeze() is the documented thaw for public payloads (GateReview.payload, #141) and determinism/provenance records that exist to be byte-for-byte reproducible, so non-deterministic ordering can break serialized-artifact comparison.", "poc": "frozenset(['alpha',1,'beta',2,'gamma']) hits the fallback (mixed str/int unorderable); across PYTHONHASHSEED=0/1/2 unfreeze returns three different orderings of the same input. A frozenset of FrozenDicts (sorted raises TypeError) is similarly non-deterministic.", "suggested_fix": "Make the fallback deterministic: return [unfreeze(v) for v in sorted(value, key=repr)] (or key=canonical_json(unfreeze(v)) for a fully type-faithful, process-stable key) and update the comment to say it sorts by a process-stable key.", "dimension": "determinism-serialization"}, {"id": "G-005", "severity": "medium", "file": "src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py", "line": "333-337", "title": "Contradicted verdict reported without any stability requirement, asymmetric with supported", "description": "In confirm_whole_corpus a 'supported' verdict requires significance in the predicted tail AND stability >= threshold across resamples, but a 'contradicted' verdict is returned as soon as p_contra <= alpha, before stability is consulted, and stability is only ever measured in the predicted (support) tail. A single noisy observed statistic landing in the opposite tail yields a hard 'contradicted' with no reproducibility check, while the symmetric 'supported' claim is gated. In the \u00a75 source-scoring path this means contradictions of external claims can be driven by non-reproducible noise, biasing alignment_rate downward (every unstable opposite-tail blip increments n_contradicted in SourceScorecard). The docstring frames this as intentional but it is a real methodological inconsistency: reproduction is demanded for one verdict polarity and not the other.", "poc": "A null centered at 0; one observed draw lands in the opposite tail by chance. confirm_whole_corpus returns CONTRADICTED ('significant in the opposite tail') without measuring whether the contradiction reproduces across resamples, whereas the same magnitude in the supportive tail would be gated by stability_threshold; in adjudication it increments scorecard.n_contradicted.", "suggested_fix": "Apply stability symmetrically: when p_contra<=alpha compute contra-tail stability and require it to clear the threshold before returning 'contradicted'; if resamples are absent return 'indeterminate (stability not assessed)' for the contradiction too. Otherwise document in METHODOLOGY.md why contradictions are held to a weaker bar and surface the missing-stability caveat in the verdict evidence.", "dimension": "methodology"}, {"id": "G-006", "severity": "medium", "file": "src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py", "line": "39-53", "title": "Direct confirm primitives default to a presence-only pre-registration gate (authority=None) \u2014 bypassable real-verification", "description": "_gate_preregistration falls back to require_locked (a pure presence check on locked_at/timestamp_proof) when authority is None, and authority defaults to None on confirm_unit_level and confirm_whole_corpus. These primitives are study-facing (the adjudication docstring instructs studies to call them inside confirm callables). A study confirmer that calls them without threading the authority gets only the sentinel check, which a hand-written timestamp_proof='x' satisfies \u2014 no content-binding, no timestamp attestation, no lock-before-confirm ordering. In the composed run_study pipeline the runner pre-verifies via require_preregistered (defense-in-depth there), but any study/test driving these primitives directly, or a future runner that forgets to pass authority, silently downgrades pre-registration to a no-op.", "poc": "Construct a Hypothesis, replace(locked_at='2020-01-01T00:00:00+00:00', timestamp_proof='not-a-real-proof'), then call confirm_whole_corpus(...) with authority omitted -> only require_locked runs -> the fake proof is accepted and a verdict is produced on a hypothesis whose lock was never cryptographically verified.", "suggested_fix": "Make authority a required keyword argument on confirm_unit_level and confirm_whole_corpus (no None default) so every confirmatory call performs full require_preregistered. If an unverified exploratory path must exist, gate it behind an explicit loudly-named flag (verify_preregistration=False), forbidden for EXTERNAL_CLAIM/DISCOVERY origins.", "dimension": "methodology"}, {"id": "G-007", "severity": "medium", "file": "scripts/capture_transcripts.py", "line": 51, "title": "Operator username PII survives transcript scrubbing outside /Users path context (committed provenance leak)", "description": "The scrubber's operator-username rule only matches the username directly after a home-path prefix: re.compile(r\"(/(?:Users|home)/)[^/\\s\\\"']+\") (confirmed at scripts/capture_transcripts.py:51). Committed transcripts embed lsof/ps output where the username appears as a bare process-owner column (e.g. 'Claude 647 thomasjones 86u IPv4 ...'); that bare form is not preceded by /Users/ or /home/, so it is never redacted. The username appears ~570 times in non-path contexts in the tracked transcripts/sessions/*.jsonl. This defeats #F-024 ('committing transcripts does not publish the operator's identity') and the ADR-0003 data-sovereignty posture for a repo intended for public release; the same gap leaves any other PII surfaced by command output unredacted.", "poc": "grep -oE '[A-Za-z]+ +[0-9]+ thomasjones' transcripts/sessions/*.jsonl prints lsof/ps lines the scrubber left intact; git ls-files --error-unmatch confirms the .jsonl is tracked/committed.", "suggested_fix": "Redact the operator username as a standalone token: derive the username at runtime (Path.home()/source dir) and add a word-boundary pattern re.compile(rf\"\\b{re.escape(user)}\\b\") in addition to the path-prefix rule, so it is masked in process listings, git config, and env dumps. Re-run the scrubber over already-committed transcripts and amend, since the leak is already in history.", "dimension": "privacy-pii"}, {"id": "G-008", "severity": "medium", "file": "src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py", "line": 85, "title": "_CONN_INIT_LOCKS and _POOLS_BY_CONN grow unboundedly \u2014 never pruned, keyed by raw conn_str", "description": "_CONN_INIT_LOCKS is a process-lifetime dict keyed by resolved conn_str with add-only lifecycle (never removed); _POOLS_BY_CONN is the same. conn_str distinctness includes any URL variation (host alias, optional params, credential rotation that changes userinfo, sslmode toggles). A long-lived process targeting many distinct DSNs (multi-tenant runner, test harness rotating DSNs, credential rotation) accumulates a Lock per distinct string and, for _POOLS_BY_CONN, a live ConnectionPool + worker thread per distinct string with no eviction \u2014 an unbounded cache and thread/connection leak. Credential rotation makes the SAME logical DB appear as a NEW conn_str, orphaning the old pool (atexit-closed but never reused/closed until exit).", "poc": "Loop setting ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL to N distinct valid loopback DSNs (e.g. differing in ?application_name=run_i) and call get_checkpointer() each iteration; len(_CONN_INIT_LOCKS) and len(_POOLS_BY_CONN) grow to N and worker-thread count climbs by one per distinct DSN, none reclaimed.", "suggested_fix": "Canonicalize the cache key (drop volatile query params / strip userinfo for the key while keeping it for connect) so cosmetic/credential changes map to one entry; cap _POOLS_BY_CONN with an LRU that pool.close()es and removes the evicted pool from _OPEN_POOLS; prune _CONN_INIT_LOCKS for keys no longer in _POOLS_BY_CONN.", "dimension": "persistence-durability"}, {"id": "G-009", "severity": "medium", "file": "src/assay_engine/pipeline.py", "line": 351, "title": "run_study never persists or correlates the provenance trail to its MLflow run (success and failure paths)", "description": "run_study records a hash-chained provenance trail and stores experiment_run_id inside the run_end entry (pipeline.py:721), but never writes the trail anywhere durable nor attaches it to the experiment run via tracker.log_artifact, even when a tracker and run_id are known. Persistence is opt-in (StudyResult.persist_trail() at :226 + a manual tracker.log_artifact). So the default governed run produces an MLflow run with metrics but no link back to the append-only trail, and the trail evaporates on process exit. The failure path is worse: the run_failed entry (pipeline.py:732) omits experiment_run_id even though run_id is in scope and the finally block terminates the MLflow run as FAILED (:754) \u2014 the case where correlation matters most leaves a FAILED run and an in-memory-only trail sharing no recorded id. GOVERNANCE \u00a73 calls the trail 'the spine of reproducibility and of the independence claim'.", "poc": "run_study(plan, gate_handler=auto_approve, tracker=MlflowExperimentTracker()): on success the MLflow run has params/metrics but zero artifacts and no provenance.json; StudyResult.provenance is gone on exit. With a discover() that raises, the run is set FAILED but run_failed has no experiment_run_id \u2014 given the FAILED run id you cannot find its trail, and vice versa.", "suggested_fix": "When a tracker and run_id are present, have run_study persist the trail to a working path and tracker.log_artifact(run_id, path) on the success path (best-effort partial trail on failure), guarded like the other track() calls. Add experiment_run_id=run_id to the run_failed trail.record() payload (deterministic, preserves hash-determinism) so failed runs are equally correlatable across the trail and the experiment store.", "dimension": "observability-provenance"}, {"id": "G-010", "severity": "medium", "file": "src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py", "line": 217, "title": "numpy resample-stability path allocates a full R\u00d7N boolean matrix (peak-memory blowup) and lacks an at-scale equivalence/memory test", "description": "The numpy branch of _resample_stability computes null_arr[None,:] >= res_arr[:,None] (and the <= variant) before .sum(axis=1), materializing a dense R\u00d7N boolean array \u2014 O(R\u00b7N) PEAK MEMORY, whereas the pure-Python fallback it claims to merely speed up is O(N). Confirmed at confirm.py:216-217. At corpus scale (R=10k resamples vs N=10k permutation null = 10^8 booleans \u2248 100 MB transient; 20k\u00d720k \u2248 400 MB) enabling the optional numpy extra silently raises peak memory by orders of magnitude and can OOM where the pure path would not, while the docstring frames it as 'purely a speed optimization, never a semantic change'. Compounding this, the only equivalence test (test_resample_stability_numpy_path_matches_pure_python, null=40/res=25) is far below any size where the mask is observable and pins no memory contract, so a future searchsorted/chunked rewrite has no at-scale guardrail.", "poc": "null=list(rng.normal(size=20000)); res=list(rng.normal(0.5,size=20000)); confirm._resample_stability(res, null, 'greater', 0.05) transiently allocates ~400MB (a 20000x20000 bool mask), whereas the pure path's working set is O(N). No existing test exercises this size or any memory characteristic.", "suggested_fix": "Sort the null once and use np.searchsorted for per-resample extreme counts in O((R+N)logN) time / O(R+N) memory: greater -> cnt = len(null) - searchsorted(null_sorted, res_arr, 'left'); less -> cnt = searchsorted(null_sorted, res_arr, 'right'); the (cnt+1)/(n+1)<=alpha test is byte-identical. If keeping the broadcast form, chunk the resamples and document the memory characteristic. Add a parametrized equivalence test at N=2000/R=2000 (and optionally a tracemalloc sub-quadratic peak-allocation regression test) so the rewrite is held to byte-identical results.", "dimension": "performance"}, {"id": "G-011", "severity": "medium", "file": "src/assay_engine/methodology/discovery.py", "line": 150, "title": "discover_and_confirm does not type-guard the confirmer's return value (asymmetric with adjudication/pipeline)", "description": "In adjudicate_with_baseline (adjudication.py:253) and the pipeline runner (pipeline.py:563) a confirmer that forgets to return (yields None) is caught with an explicit isinstance(verdict, Verdict) guard (#F-021). The standalone discovery runner was left out: after verdict = confirm(hypothesis, held_out) (discovery.py:150) it goes straight to verdict.hypothesis_id (:151, which only checks id-mismatch, not type). A confirmer returning None raises an opaque AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'hypothesis_id' instead of the documented typed FirewallViolation. The module docstring (lines 94-97) promises FirewallViolation on a verdict<->hypothesis misattribution, and a None verdict never reaches that check, breaking the typed-exception contract for one of the two public Firewall-B entry points.", "poc": "def bad_confirm(hyp, held_out): pass  # returns None. discover_and_confirm(corpus, split, discover=..., confirm=bad_confirm, authority=...) raises AttributeError(\"'NoneType' object has no attribute 'hypothesis_id'\") instead of FirewallViolation('confirm returned NoneType ... expected a Verdict').", "suggested_fix": "Mirror the adjudication/pipeline guard immediately after confirm(): if not isinstance(verdict, Verdict): raise FirewallViolation(f\"confirm returned {type(verdict).__name__} for hypothesis {hypothesis.hypothesis_id!r} \u2014 expected a Verdict\"). Verdict is already imported (line 39).", "dimension": "error-contract"}, {"id": "G-012", "severity": "medium", "file": "src/assay_engine/provenance.py", "line": 266, "title": "from_records raises raw KeyError on a malformed record instead of the typed ProvenanceError", "description": "from_records rebuilds ProvenanceEntry by directly subscripting each record (r['seq'], r['kind'], r['summary'], r['payload'], r['timestamp'], r['prev_hash'], r['entry_hash'] at provenance.py:266-275, confirmed). A record deserialized from an external/persistent store missing any key raises a bare KeyError, not the module's typed ProvenanceError. Every other deserialization/verification path here surfaces ProvenanceError (verify_records, _digest, clock/timestamp guards, freeze guard). The trail is explicitly a trust-boundary deserialization point ('rebuild entries ... and verify the chain'), so an externally-supplied record dropping/renaming a field should fail with the trail's typed error catchable alongside verify_records failures; a non-int seq / non-str field would also propagate untyped.", "poc": "from_records([{ 'seq':0,'kind':'x','summary':'s','payload':{}, 'timestamp':'2020-01-01T00:00:00+00:00','prev_hash':'assay-provenance:v1:genesis' }]) -> KeyError: 'entry_hash' (expected ProvenanceError describing the malformed record).", "suggested_fix": "Wrap the construction in try/except (KeyError, TypeError, ValueError) as exc: raise ProvenanceError(f'provenance record could not be rebuilt: {exc}') from None, then call verify_records on the typed-good entries.", "dimension": "error-contract"}, {"id": "G-013", "severity": "medium", "file": "tests/test_baseline.py", "line": 237, "title": "test_cosine_matrix_scale_uses_fast_path passes even if the numpy fast path is reverted to the pure O(n^2*d) path", "description": "The test claims to guard against 'a regression to the pure O(n^2*d) path at corpus scale', but its only fast-path-discriminating assertion is assert elapsed < 2.0; the rest (len==300, m[0][0]==1.0) hold for either path. The pure-Python _cosine_matrix_pure on a 300x64 matrix completes in ~0.08s \u2014 ~24x under the 2.0s threshold. Reverting cosine_similarity_matrix (primitives.py:155) to always call _cosine_matrix_pure leaves this test GREEN. The test does not discriminate the fix it names, and the loose wall-clock budget would not catch a real regression on a larger corpus either (and is flaky on loaded CI).", "poc": "Change cosine_similarity_matrix (primitives.py:153-155) to unconditionally return _cosine_matrix_pure(rows); the test still passes (300x64 pure runtime ~0.08s < 2.0s, m[0][0]==1.0, dims 300x300).", "suggested_fix": "Assert the fast path is taken rather than timing it: monkeypatch _cosine_matrix_pure to record/raise and assert it is NOT called when numpy is installed, or spy on _cosine_matrix_numpy and assert it WAS called. Drop the brittle non-discriminating elapsed < 2.0 assertion.", "dimension": "test-quality"}, {"id": "G-014", "severity": "medium", "file": "tests/test_observability.py", "line": 162, "title": "test_install_flush_on_exit_wires_atexit_and_sigterm passes even if the SIGTERM handler wiring (#O3) is fully reverted", "description": "The test asserts BOTH atexit and the SIGTERM handler are wired, but the SIGTERM half is asserted only inside `if \"h\" in captured:` (tests/test_observability.py:188-190). captured['h'] is populated solely as a side effect of the source calling signal.signal(SIGTERM, _handler) (tracing.py:141). If a developer reverts the SIGTERM block (tracing.py:131-141) keeping only atexit.register (:130), signal.signal is never invoked, captured stays empty, the if-branch is skipped, and the test passes on the atexit assertions alone. The SIGTERM portion of this regression guard is vacuous on the exact revert it is meant to catch \u2014 the conditional turns a should-be-required assertion into a no-op when the fix is missing.", "poc": "Delete the `if threading.current_thread() is threading.main_thread(): ... signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, _handler)` block (tracing.py:131-141), keep atexit.register(lambda: _safe(flush)). The test still passes: atexit handler registered and flush>=1; captured is empty so the SIGTERM assertions are skipped.", "suggested_fix": "Since pytest runs the body on the main thread, unconditionally assert 'h' in captured and exercise it: captured['h'](signal.SIGTERM, None); assert calls['shutdown'] >= 1. Remove the if-guard so a reverted handler fails the test.", "dimension": "test-quality"}, {"id": "G-015", "severity": "medium", "file": ".github/workflows/ci.yml", "line": 135, "title": "SBOM step re-runs pip-audit without the allowlist, so an accepted advisory fails CI", "description": "The audit job's vuln gate (ci.yml:113-127) builds an IGNORES array from .pip-audit-ignore and passes --ignore-vuln to pip-audit. The next step 'SBOM (CycloneDX) from the lockfile' (ci.yml:135) runs pip-audit ... --format cyclonedx-json under set -euo pipefail WITHOUT any --ignore-vuln args (confirmed). pip-audit exits non-zero on any found vulnerability regardless of output format, so any advisory the team has consciously allowlisted passes the gate step but FAILS the SBOM step \u2014 bricking CI on exactly the condition the allowlist exists to handle, and never emitting the SBOM when it is most needed. The allowlist is effectively a no-op once any accepted advisory is added.", "poc": "Add a real advisory id (present for a requirements.lock-pinned package) to .pip-audit-ignore. The 'pip-audit (reproducible known-vulnerability scan)' step passes (allowlisted); the 'SBOM' step runs pip-audit without --ignore-vuln, exits 1 on the same advisory, set -e aborts, the audit job fails, and all-checks blocks the merge.", "suggested_fix": "Factor the IGNORES array into a shared function and reuse it in the SBOM invocation, or decouple SBOM generation from the vuln scan (cyclonedx-py requirements requirements.lock, or pass --ignore-vuln to the cyclonedx invocation). The SBOM should describe the dependency set independent of accepted advisories.", "dimension": "ci-supply-chain"}, {"id": "G-016", "severity": "medium", "file": ".github/dependabot.yml", "line": 8, "title": "Dependabot bumps pyproject ranges but cannot regenerate the hash-pinned lockfiles the CI gate enforces, so its PRs fail the lockfile-sync gate; uv.lock is unmanaged", "description": "The pip ecosystem entry lets Dependabot bump pyproject.toml constraints, but the audit job (ci.yml:98-112) enforces that requirements.lock and requirements-core.lock are byte-for-byte regenerable from pyproject via uv pip compile. Dependabot has no hook to run uv pip compile, so any Dependabot PR changing a pyproject constraint leaves the committed lockfiles stale and FAILS the lockfile-sync gate -> all-checks blocks the merge -> the dependency-update PRs are unmergeable without manual intervention every time. Most runtime deps live in optional-extras with floors-only/range pins (langgraph>=1.2,<2), so Dependabot may not even open PRs while installed transitive versions drift. Additionally uv.lock is committed but referenced by nothing in CI (the gate compiles to requirements*.lock), an orphaned unverified lock artifact that can diverge unnoticed.", "poc": "Wait for (or simulate) a weekly Dependabot pip PR bumping a pyproject constraint without regenerating locks. The 'Lockfiles are in sync with pyproject' step diffs requirements.lock against a fresh uv pip compile and exits 1 ('::error::requirements.lock is stale'); all-checks fails; the PR cannot merge.", "suggested_fix": "Either add a CI/scheduled post-update step (or Dependabot-compatible action) that runs uv pip compile for both lockfiles and commits the result, or switch the supply-chain workflow to manage/audit uv.lock directly (uv-native) and drop the orphaned lock. Document which lock is canonical and enforce exactly one; consider versioning-strategy/grouping to batch lock regeneration.", "dimension": "ci-supply-chain"}, {"id": "G-017", "severity": "low", "file": "src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py", "line": "162-168", "title": "Unit-level confirm validates predicted_direction shape but never uses it, leaving direction_supports_claim unverified", "description": "confirm_unit_level re-validates that hypothesis.predicted_direction is in {'greater','less'} as defense-in-depth, but the unit-level verdict is decided entirely by the trusted caller flag direction_supports_claim and never cross-checked against the pre-registered predicted_direction. A hypothesis locked with predicted_direction='greater' can be confirmed 'supported' on direction_supports_claim=True even when the held-out statistic points the other way \u2014 the engine binds a direction into the proof then ignores it at decision time. The validation at lines 162-168 gives a false sense the locked direction governs the verdict; it does not. Smaller than G-001 (requires a dishonest caller flag rather than free post-hoc choice) but the locked direction provides zero decision-time guarantee for the unit-level path.", "poc": "Hypothesis locked predicted_direction='greater'. const VERDICT_SCHEMA = {
  type: 'object',
  required: ['finding_id', 'confirmed', 'reasoning', 'severity_assessment'],
  properties: {
    finding_id: { type: 'string' },
    confirmed: { type: 'boolean' },
    reasoning: { type: 'string' },
    severity_assessment: { type: 'string', enum: ['critical', 'high', 'medium', 'low', 'false_positive'] },
  },
}
const vprompt = (f, n) =>
  `You are verifier #${n}, independent. Do NOT optimistically confirm. Read the ACTUAL code at ${ROOT} and independently determine whether this is a TRUE production-readiness problem in the CURRENT code (3 hardening passes already applied).\n\nFinding ${f.id}: ${f.title}\nFile: ${f.file}:${f.line}\nDescription: ${f.description}\nPoC: ${f.poc}\nSuggested fix: ${f.suggested_fix}\n\nConfirmed=true ONLY if the problem exists as described in current code, the PoC is reproducible, and it is a genuine concern (not speculative, not already-handled, not intended design). Be concrete and cite file:line.`

phase('Verify')
const results = await pipeline(
  FINDINGS,
  f => parallel([
    () => agent(vprompt(f, 1), { label: 'vA:' + f.id, phase: 'Verify', schema: VERDICT_SCHEMA }),
    () => agent(vprompt(f, 2), { label: 'vB:' + f.id, phase: 'Verify', schema: VERDICT_SCHEMA }),
  ]),
  (vs, f) => {
    const [a, b] = vs.filter(Boolean)
    const both = a && b && a.confirmed && b.confirmed
    const neither = (!a || !a.confirmed) && (!b || !b.confirmed)
    return {
      id: f.id, severity: f.severity, file: f.file, line: f.line, title: f.title,
      description: f.description, poc: f.poc, suggested_fix: f.suggested_fix, dimension: f.dimension,
      both_confirmed: both, neither_confirmed: neither, split: !both && !neither,
      a: a ? { c: a.confirmed, s: a.severity_assessment, why: a.reasoning } : null,
      b: b ? { c: b.confirmed, s: b.severity_assessment, why: b.reasoning } : null,
    }
  }
)
phase('Triage')
const confirmed = results.filter(Boolean).filter(r => r.both_confirmed)
const rejected = results.filter(Boolean).filter(r => r.neither_confirmed)
const split = results.filter(Boolean).filter(r => r.split)
log('confirmed=' + confirmed.length + ' rejected=' + rejected.length + ' split=' + split.length)
return { confirmed, rejected, split, all: results.filter(Boolean) }

confirmed.title: Post-hoc direction selection (HARKing) for whole-corpus hypotheses locked with predicted_direction=None
confirmed.description: _resolve_direction accepts a confirm-time predicted_direction whenever the locked hypothesis has predicted_direction=None, and nothing forces a WHOLE_CORPUS hypothesis to lock a non-None direction. A study can pre-register a directionless whole-corpus hypothesis, inspect the observed statistic/baseline, then choose 'greater' or 'less' at confirm_whole_corpus time to land in whichever tail is significant — textbook HARKing that defeats the pre-registration binding (issue #24 the docstring claims to close). Verified in source: when locked_dir is None and predicted_direction is None it raises, but when predicted_direction is supplied it is used as the resolved tail, so the verdict polarity is chosen post-hoc on a hypothesis whose cryptographic lock binds 'no direction'.
confirmed.poc: Lock a WHOLE_CORPUS Hypothesis with predicted_direction=None via lock_hypothesis (proof binds None). After observing obs=95.0 against a 0..99 null, call confirm_whole_corpus(..., predicted_direction='greater', authority=auth) -> 'supported'; had obs been low, pass 'less' instead. Pre-registration verification passes; tail chosen after seeing data.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Require predicted_direction at lock time for WHOLE_CORPUS hypotheses (raise in Hypothesis.__post_init__ when kind is WHOLE_CORPUS and direction is None), or in _resolve_direction reject locked_dir is None for any hypothesis going through require_preregistered (accept a confirm-time direction only for unlocked exploratory hypotheses). The directionless-deferred path must be unreachable for a pre-registered confirmatory test.

a.why:
Confirmed as a true defect in current code (3 passes applied). Trace:

1. Hypothesis.__post_init__ (src/assay_engine/methodology/hypothesis.py:80-97) explicitly permits predicted_direction=None for ANY kind including WHOLE_CORPUS. The inline comment at lines 86-87 states None is "deferred to confirm time on the whole-corpus path" — i.e. the directionless-deferred path is intentional and reachable, with no guard requiring WHOLE_CORPUS to lock a non-None direction.

2. canonical_hypothesis_digest (preregistration.py:109-119) includes predicted_direction in the digest. Locking a None-direction hypothesis binds "None" into the proof; verify_preregistration (preregistration.py:222-257) only checks the proof matches the CURRENT content (still None), so it passes. The cryptographic binding is satisfied precisely because no direction was committed.

3. confirm_whole_corpus (confirm.py:289-303) runs _gate_preregistration (which only verifies the lock/timestamp) then calls _resolve_direction.

4. _resolve_direction (confirm.py:238-252): when locked_dir is None and a confirm-time predicted_direction is supplied, it returns the confirm-time value (line 252) with NO re-validation against the lock. The verdict polarity (p_support vs p_contradict / supported vs contradicted, confirm.py:304-337) is then computed from a tail chosen after observing the data.

PoC is reproducible exactly as described: lock a WHOLE_CORPUS hypothesis with predicted_direction=None, observe obs=95 vs a 0..99 null, pass predicted_direction='greater' -> 'supported'; had obs been low, pass 'less'. All pre-registration gates pass. This is textbook HARKing/post-hoc tail selection.

This directly contradicts the engine's stated guarantee: docstrings at confirm.py:231 ("Direction is fixed at lock time on the hypothesis (issue #24)") and confirm.py:277 ("The predicted tail is taken from the hypothesis's pre-registered predicted_direction") are FALSE for the None-locked path. The methodology engine exists to prevent exactly this; the deferred-None path is a sanctioned escape hatch that defeats the pre-registration binding for confirmatory whole-corpus tests.

Severity high rather than critical: it requires the study author to deliberately lock a directionless hypothesis (not a silent/default-triggered bug), and the suggested fix is straightforward (require predicted_direction at lock time for WHOLE_CORPUS in __post_init__, or in _resolve_direction reject locked_dir is None for any pre-registered/gated confirmation). But it is a genuine, non-speculative integrity hole in the core scientific-validity contract, so it warrants more than medium.

b.why:
Confirmed as a true, current, reproducible methodology-integrity defect (HARKing) in the post-3-pass code.

Mechanism verified in source:
- src/assay_engine/methodology/hypothesis.py:80-97 — Hypothesis.__post_init__ permits predicted_direction=None for ANY kind, including WHOLE_CORPUS. The inline comment at lines 86-87 explicitly endorses "None (deferred to confirm time on the whole-corpus path)". There is no guard requiring a non-None direction for WHOLE_CORPUS.
- src/assay_engine/methodology/preregistration.py:117 — canonical_hypothesis_digest binds predicted_direction into the proof, so locking with None cryptographically attests "no direction"; the proof still verifies later when a confirm-time direction is supplied.
- src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:230-258 — _resolve_direction: when locked_dir is None (line 238-239 false), it only raises if the confirm-time predicted_direction is ALSO None (line 246-247); otherwise the else branch (line 251-252) accepts the confirm-time direction as the resolved tail. confirm_whole_corpus (line 303) then uses this for the verdict.

PoC reproduced (run with the FULL pre-registration gate via authority=auth, i.e. require_preregistered content-binding + timestamp + ordering, NOT the weak presence gate):
  h = WHOLE_CORPUS, predicted_direction=None; locked via lock_hypothesis (proof binds None and verifies).
  null = 0..99.
  confirm_whole_corpus(observed=200, resamples=[200]*50, predicted_direction='greater', authority=auth) -> label 'supported'.
  confirm_whole_corpus(observed=-100, resamples=[-100]*50, predicted_direction='less', authority=auth) -> label 'supported'.
The identical locked, proof-verified hypothesis yields 'supported' in EITHER tail purely by choosing the confirm-time direction after observing the data — textbook HARKing that defeats the pre-registration binding the confirm.py:231 docstring claims to close (issue #24). (An earlier run with non-extreme obs returned 'indeterminate' only because the resample-stability threshold wasn't met; with a reproducible extreme effect it lands 'supported' in whichever tail the analyst picks.)

This is not speculative, not already-handled, and contradicts the module's own stated guarantee ("Direction is fixed at lock time ... so it cannot be chosen post-hoc"). The directionless-deferred path is reachable for a fully pre-registered confirmatory test, so the pre-registration binding is bypassable. Suggested fix in the finding is sound: forbid predicted_direction=None for WHOLE_CORPUS at construction/lock, or make _resolve_direction reject locked_dir is None for any pre-registered (require_preregistered) hypothesis, accepting a confirm-time direction only for unlocked exploratory use.

Severity: high — it is a methodology-correctness/integrity defect in the core confirmatory engine of a tool whose entire value proposition is rigorous, tamper-evident pre-registration; it silently converts a directionless pre-registration into a guaranteed-significant verdict. It is not 'critical' in the code-safety/RCE sense and requires the study author to author a directionless WHOLE_CORPUS hypothesis, but it directly undermines the headline scientific guarantee.

confirmed.title: Bootstrap lock_timeout session-GUC leaks into pooled connection used for all later checkpoint writes
confirmed.description: _acquire_migration_lock runs SELECT set_config('lock_timeout', %s, false) — is_local=false, i.e. SESSION scope (confirmed at source line ~210). The connection comes from `with pool.connection() as conn:` and is returned to the same pool on block exit; psycopg_pool does not reset session state without a reset= callback (none configured). That pool is then used by PostgresSaver for every subsequent checkpoint read/write, so the bootstrap-only lock_timeout (default 30s) silently persists on a long-lived pooled connection. Under lock contention a normal checkpoint write that would otherwise block briefly aborts with 'canceling statement due to lock_timeout' — durability loss at exactly the interrupt/resume the checkpointer exists to guarantee.
confirmed.poc: With ASSAY_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS=30000, run get_checkpointer() to bootstrap, then re-check-out a connection and assert conn.execute('SHOW lock_timeout').fetchone() != '0'. Two concurrent resumes contending on the same checkpoint row produce a spurious 'canceling statement due to lock_timeout' instead of completing.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Reset the GUC on the same connection before block exit after setup()/advisory-unlock (conn.execute("SELECT set_config('lock_timeout','0',false)") or RESET lock_timeout in finally); or configure ConnectionPool(reset=lambda c: c.execute('RESET ALL')); or run bootstrap on a dedicated one-shot psycopg.connect() that is closed rather than returned to the pool.

a.why: Confirmed in current code. checkpoint.py:212 runs `SELECT set_config('lock_timeout', %s, false)` with is_local=false (SESSION scope), not transaction-local. The connection is obtained via `with pool.connection() as conn:` (line 283) from a ConnectionPool constructed at lines 266-278 with NO `reset=` callback. psycopg_pool's default connection reset does not issue RESET ALL — with autocommit=True there is no transaction to roll back and no reset callback, so the SESSION-scoped lock_timeout persists when the connection returns to the pool. That same pool is wrapped in PostgresSaver at line 299 and used for all subsequent checkpoint reads/writes. Default ASSAY_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS=30000 (line 61) makes the `if timeout_ms:` guard (line 211) true by default, so the leak occurs in the default configuration. With default min_size=1 (line 263) the bootstrap connection IS the long-lived pooled connection serving subsequent traffic. Under lock contention a checkpoint write that would normally block briefly aborts with 'canceling statement due to lock_timeout' — durability loss at the interrupt/resume the checkpointer exists to guarantee, contradicting the module's own stated invariant (docstring lines 13-17). The advisory lock auto-releases on connection return, but lock_timeout does not. PoC (bootstrap, re-checkout, SHOW lock_timeout != '0') is reproducible given a live Postgres. Not speculative, not already-handled, not intended design. Could not execute the PoC directly because psycopg_pool (the optional 'persistence' extra) is not installed in this environment; verification rests on the source code and documented psycopg_pool reset semantics. Rated high rather than critical because it surfaces only under lock contention, but it directly undermines the durability guarantee. Valid fix options: RESET lock_timeout in the finally block on the same connection, configure ConnectionPool(reset=...), or bootstrap on a one-shot psycopg.connect() that is closed.

b.why: Verified against current code at /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py. Line 212: conn.execute("SELECT set_config('lock_timeout', %s, false)", ...) sets lock_timeout with is_local=false (SESSION scope). The connection is autocommit=True (line 271), so it cannot be transaction-scoped anyway. The connection is obtained via `with pool.connection() as conn:` (line 283) and returns to the pool on block exit. The ConnectionPool constructor (lines 266-278) configures no reset= callback; psycopg_pool's default putconn behavior rolls back open transactions but does NOT RESET session GUCs, so lock_timeout persists. The finally block (lines 290-296) only runs pg_advisory_unlock — it does not reset lock_timeout. That same pool is then cached (line 298) and wrapped in PostgresSaver for every subsequent checkpoint read/write (lines 245, 254, 299). With the default min_size=1 (line 263), the leaked connection is the single persistent pooled connection, making reproduction reliable. A grep confirms no lock_timeout reset and no reset= callback anywhere in src/ or tests/; no test covers cleanup of the GUC (tests only assert it is SET, lines 481/677). Consequence matches description: a contended checkpoint write aborts with 'canceling statement due to lock_timeout' instead of waiting — durability loss at the exact interrupt/resume the checkpointer exists to guarantee (the failure the module docstring lines 13-17 claims to prevent). This is genuine, not speculative, not already-handled, not intended design. Severity is high rather than critical because: lock_timeout is only triggered under actual row-lock contention (e.g., two concurrent resumes on the same checkpoint), it is a non-default code path (requires ASSAY_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS != 0, though 30000 is the default), and it degrades but does not silently corrupt — the failed write raises rather than persisting bad state.

confirmed.title: BULK tier has no inner cancellation (asymmetric with HIGH_STAKES) — a hung backend permanently leaks a pool slot and drives endless retryable backpressure
confirmed.description: Confirmed in source: HIGH_STAKES wraps its stream in `anyio.fail_after(HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT)` (seam.py:438) so a hung subprocess is cancelled inside the worker and the bounded-pool slot is freed. BULK has no equivalent — it relies only on client_kwargs={'timeout': BULK_TIMEOUT} reaching httpx (seam.py:349) and the outer _with_timeout(..., BULK_TIMEOUT+10) (seam.py:510), which by its own docstring cannot interrupt a thread blocked in a backend call. If the httpx timeout does not fire (wedged stream, proxy holding the socket, ChatOllama not propagating client_kwargs, C-level blocking read) the worker is stuck forever and its _inflight slot never releases. _submit_bounded reports saturation as a RETRYABLE RateLimitError ('pool saturated', seam.py:223) and _run_with_retries treats it as transient backpressure, so once enough BULK workers wedge (up to _POOL_WORKERS=8) every future call livelocks on saturation backpressure that never clears.
confirmed.poc: Monkeypatch _bulk_complete to block with no timeout (threading.Event().wait()). Submit _POOL_WORKERS BULK requests via _with_timeout; each returns FuturesTimeout to the caller but the worker stays alive holding its slot. After 8, every _submit_bounded raises RateLimitError('saturated') indefinitely; slots never recover for the rest of process life.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Give BULK the same in-worker hard bound as HIGH_STAKES (run the generation under anyio.fail_after(BULK_TIMEOUT) via an async ollama client, or a cancellable subprocess). At minimum add a watchdog that surfaces a PermanentReasoningError ('reasoning pool wedged') on saturation that never clears rather than infinite RateLimitError backpressure, and assert ChatOllama honors client_kwargs timeout on the streaming path.

a.why: Verified against current code at /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py. The asymmetry is real: HIGH_STAKES wraps its stream in `with anyio.fail_after(HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT)` (seam.py:438) inside `_drain`, which truly cancels query() and tears down the child subprocess, freeing the pool slot. BULK's `_bulk_complete` (319-383) calls `client.invoke(messages)` synchronously at line 363 with NO cancellation wrapper — it relies only on `client_kwargs={"timeout": BULK_TIMEOUT}` (349) and the outer `_with_timeout(..., BULK_TIMEOUT+10)` (510). The outer `_with_timeout` provably cannot interrupt a wedged worker: its own comment at seam.py:259-261 states "a ThreadPoolExecutor cannot interrupt a thread already blocked in a backend call. The worker keeps its slot until the call returns." So if the httpx timeout fails to fire (wedged stream, proxy holding the socket, ChatOllama not propagating client_kwargs, or a C-level blocking read), the BULK slot leaks permanently. `_submit_bounded` (218-226) raises a RETRYABLE RateLimitError once `_inflight >= _POOL_WORKERS=8`. The maintainers themselves acknowledge this exact residual risk in the BULK_NUM_PREDICT comment (seam.py:95-98): "the wall-clock timeout cannot interrupt a call already blocked in the backend (a leaked pool slot)." BULK_NUM_PREDICT guards runaway *generation length* but not a socket/C-level wedge. The PoC (monkeypatch _bulk_complete to block on threading.Event().wait(), submit 8 BULK requests) is reproducible: each caller gets a FuturesTimeout while the worker stays alive holding its slot, and after 8 every subsequent submit hits the saturation guard, with slots never recovering for process life.\n\nTwo inaccuracies in the finding's framing that do NOT negate it: (1) the finding calls the backpressure "infinite"/"livelock indefinitely." It is not infinite — `_run_with_retries` caps rate-limit retries at RATE_LIMIT_MAX_RETRIES (default 5) at seam.py:575-576 and re-raises, and RUN_JSON_DEADLINE/deadline bound wall-clock. The accurate description is a PERMANENT process-wide reasoning outage surfaced as a RateLimitError on every call (after 5 bounded retries), with slots never recovering — which matches the finding's own end-state claim ("slots never recover for the rest of process life"). (2) The trigger requires the precondition that the httpx timeout does not fire; in the common case it does. But that precondition is realistic for a local Ollama runtime under crash/load, and the whole point of HIGH_STAKES's inner fail_after is that the maintainers already deemed this class of leak worth a hard in-worker bound — leaving BULK without the equivalent is a genuine, unintended asymmetry, not intended design. The suggested fix (give BULK an in-worker hard bound equivalent to HIGH_STAKES, e.g. run generation under anyio.fail_after via an async client, or surface PermanentReasoningError on never-clearing saturation) is correct and addresses the root cause.

b.why:
TRUE production-readiness defect, independently confirmed by reading current code and a reproduced PoC.

Asymmetry is real (cited): HIGH_STAKES wraps its stream in `with anyio.fail_after(HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT): await _drain_stream()` (seam.py:438) — a hard in-worker cancellation added in pass 2 (#127) precisely to free a leaked pool slot. BULK (`_bulk_complete`, seam.py:319-383) has NO equivalent: its synchronous `client.invoke(messages)` (seam.py:363) is protected only by `client_kwargs={"timeout": BULK_TIMEOUT}` (seam.py:349) and the outer `_with_timeout(..., BULK_TIMEOUT+10)` (seam.py:510). `_with_timeout`'s own docstring (seam.py:258-262) states a ThreadPoolExecutor cannot interrupt a thread blocked in a backend call and that it does NOT call future.cancel().

The unbounded path is concrete, not speculative. In the installed/pinned stack (langchain-ollama 1.1.0, ollama 0.6.2, httpx 0.28.1): client_kwargs DOES propagate (ChatOllama line 956 `Client(host=..., **sync_client_kwargs)` -> ollama BaseClient `httpx.Client(timeout=timeout)` at _client.py:112-115), so the finding's "ChatOllama not propagating client_kwargs" sub-hypothesis is actually false here. BUT the deeper problem holds: ChatOllama's `invoke()`/`_generate` forces `"stream": kwargs.pop("stream", True)` (chat_models.py:800), so even a non-streaming call goes through `self._client.chat(stream=True)` -> ollama `_request` uses `self._client.stream(...)` + `r.iter_lines()` (_client.py:183-190). httpx applies the scalar timeout as a PER-READ `read` timeout (verified: httpx.Timeout(120) -> read=120 applied per body read), NOT a total-stream wall clock. A wedged/slow-dribbling backend or interposed proxy that emits any byte within each BULK_TIMEOUT window keeps the worker alive indefinitely; `num_predict=2048` bounds model tokens but not non-token bytes (keepalive/whitespace/proxy chunking). There is no inner cancellation, so the worker outlives `BULK_TIMEOUT+10` and the slot is never released.

PoC reproduced (monkeypatched `_bulk_complete` to block, as the finding specifies): filling all _POOL_WORKERS=8 slots pins `_inflight=8/8` permanently; an unrelated healthy BULK call then fails with `RateLimitError: reasoning timeout pool saturated (8/8 in-flight)` after exhausting the rate-limit budget; `_inflight` stays at 8 (permanent leak, recovery only by restart). _submit_bounded (seam.py:223) classifies saturation as a RETRYABLE RateLimitError and _run_with_retries (seam.py:574-578) treats it as transient backpressure, so a permanently-wedged pool is indistinguishable from transient pressure — every subsequent call of any tier degrades, with no PermanentReasoningError('pool wedged') signal. This is the residual half of the exact defect PASS-2 confirmed as HIGH and fixed for HIGH_STAKES.

Severity HIGH (not critical): it requires a degraded dependency (wedged/dribbling backend or proxy) rather than firing under normal operation, and each individual caller still receives an error rather than blocking forever — but once triggered the shared module-global pool is permanently and process-wide degraded across all studies/tiers with no recovery short of restart. The suggested fix (give BULK an in-worker hard bound, or at minimum surface a PermanentReasoningError when saturation never clears, plus assert the streaming timeout) is the right shape.

confirmed.title: unfreeze() promises 'stable order for reproducible output' for frozensets but the unorderable-element fallback iterates in non-deterministic order

Frozenset iteration order for str elements varies with PYTHONHASHSEED and for FrozenDict/object elements with id-based hashing varies per run, so output order is NOT reproducible — contradicting both the docstring and the comment (the F-032 class). unfreeze() is the documented thaw for public payloads (GateReview.payload, #141) and determinism/provenance records that exist to be byte-for-byte reproducible, so non-deterministic ordering can break serialized-artifact comparison.

confirmed.title: Contradicted verdict reported without any stability requirement, asymmetric with supported
confirmed.description: In confirm_whole_corpus a 'supported' verdict requires significance in the predicted tail AND stability >= threshold across resamples, but a 'contradicted' verdict is returned as soon as p_contra <= alpha, before stability is consulted, and stability is only ever measured in the predicted (support) tail. A single noisy observed statistic landing in the opposite tail yields a hard 'contradicted' with no reproducibility check, while the symmetric 'supported' claim is gated. In the §5 source-scoring path this means contradictions of external claims can be driven by non-reproducible noise, biasing alignment_rate downward (every unstable opposite-tail blip increments n_contradicted in SourceScorecard). The docstring frames this as intentional but it is a real methodological inconsistency: reproduction is demanded for one verdict polarity and not the other.
confirmed.poc: A null centered at 0; one observed draw lands in the opposite tail by chance. confirm_whole_corpus returns CONTRADICTED ('significant in the opposite tail') without measuring whether the contradiction reproduces across resamples, whereas the same magnitude in the supportive tail would be gated by stability_threshold; in adjudication it increments scorecard.n_contradicted.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Apply stability symmetrically: when p_contra<=alpha compute contra-tail stability and require it to clear the threshold before returning 'contradicted'; if resamples are absent return 'indeterminate (stability not assessed)' for the contradiction too. Otherwise document in METHODOLOGY.md why contradictions are held to a weaker bar and surface the missing-stability caveat in the verdict evidence.

a.why:
Confirmed in current code at /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py. The asymmetry is exactly as described and is reproducible.

Code facts (confirm.py):
- Line 334-337: `if p_contra <= alpha: return Verdict.contradicted(...)` fires BEFORE any stability check. The contradiction branch precedes both the `stability is None` gate (338) and the `not stable` gate (345).
- Line 315: stability is computed only for `direction` (the predicted/support tail) via `_resample_stability(resample_statistics, null_distribution, direction, alpha)`. It is never computed for `opposite` (line 304). So contra-tail reproducibility is never measured at all.
- Line 345-350: the symmetric `supported` verdict requires `stable` (stability >= threshold), and line 338-344 makes `supported` unavailable when no resamples are supplied. The support polarity is gated; the contra polarity is not.

PoC (run with .venv/bin/python, PYTHONPATH=src) reproduces it precisely. Same magnitude, same unstable noise resamples (stability=0.04, far below the 0.9 threshold):
- observed=-3.0 (opposite tail) -> CONTRADICTED, notes 'significant in the opposite tail' (no stability gate; the 0.04 stability is computed for the wrong tail and ignored).
- observed=+3.0 (predicted tail) -> INDETERMINATE, notes 'unstable across resamples'.
- With no resamples at all: contradiction still fires CONTRADICTED; support returns INDETERMINATE 'stability not assessed'.

Downstream impact confirmed: adjudication.py:83-100 `_score` increments `n_contradicted` from VerdictLabel.CONTRADICTED and computes `alignment_rate = n_supported / (n_supported + n_contradicted)`. A non-reproducible opposite-tail blip therefore increments n_contradicted and biases alignment_rate (a published SourceScorecard metric, METHODOLOGY.md §5) downward, with no reproducibility check applied to that contradiction. pipeline.py:673-685 logs these as run metrics.

Not already-handled: the only mitigation is the docstring note (lines 283, 333) stating contradictions are reportable without stability. That is a function-level acknowledgement, not a methodology-level justification. METHODOLOGY.md §4 (lines 79-89) frames Supported and Contradicted symmetrically ('at the pre-specified threshold' for both) and explicitly cautions that disagreement is not automatically proof a claim is wrong — which is the rationale for the indeterminate verdict and which the contradiction path bypasses. The finding's core point (reproduction demanded for one polarity, not the other, biasing a real output metric) is therefore a genuine methodological inconsistency, not speculative and not fully intended/justified.

Severity medium rather than high: the behavior is reproducible and biases a core published metric, but (a) it requires the specific noisy-opposite-tail scenario to manifest, (b) it is partially documented as a deliberate choice in the function docstring, and (c) it is a correctness/methodology bias, not a crash, data-loss, or security issue. The finding's suggested fix (symmetric contra-tail stability gate, or an explicit METHODOLOGY.md justification + missing-stability caveat in the verdict evidence) is appropriate.

confirmed.title: Direct confirm primitives default to a presence-only pre-registration gate (authority=None) — bypassable real-verification
confirmed.description: _gate_preregistration falls back to require_locked (a pure presence check on locked_at/timestamp_proof) when authority is None, and authority defaults to None on confirm_unit_level and confirm_whole_corpus. These primitives are study-facing (the adjudication docstring instructs studies to call them inside confirm callables). A study confirmer that calls them without threading the authority gets only the sentinel check, which a hand-written timestamp_proof='x' satisfies — no content-binding, no timestamp attestation, no lock-before-confirm ordering. In the composed run_study pipeline the runner pre-verifies via require_preregistered (defense-in-depth there), but any study/test driving these primitives directly, or a future runner that forgets to pass authority, silently downgrades pre-registration to a no-op.

confirmed.suggested_fix: Make authority a required keyword argument on confirm_unit_level and confirm_whole_corpus (no None default) so every confirmatory call performs full require_preregistered. If an unverified exploratory path must exist, gate it behind an explicit loudly-named flag (verify_preregistration=False), forbidden for EXTERNAL_CLAIM/DISCOVERY origins.

The MlflowExperimentTracker DOES expose log_artifact (observability/tracking.py:92-93), so attachment is technically available but never invoked.
2. The success terminus run_end (lines 717-723) records experiment_run_id=run_id, deterministically linking the trail to the MLflow run.
3. The failure terminus run_failed (lines 731-737) records ONLY error_type/message/last_phase and OMITS experiment_run_id, even though run_id is in scope (declared :311, set :353) and the finally block marks the MLflow run FAILED (line 754). On failure the except block re-raises (line 748), so NO StudyResult is returned — the only surviving artifact is the caller-owned partial trail, whose run_failed entry has no recorded link to the FAILED MLflow run id. This is a genuine, reproducible asymmetry exactly as the PoC describes.

NUANCE / why LOW not higher:
- The "trail evaporates / no durable persistence by default" framing (Claim A) is INTENDED, documented design, not a defect. GOVERNANCE.md §3 (lines 102-105) explicitly states "An in-memory trail is still as trustworthy as its process" and that a caller may pass its own trail so a raising run leaves an auditable partial trail; the persist_trail docstring (lines 229-233) explicitly delegates durability and log_artifact attachment to the caller. So default in-memory behavior and caller-owned persistence is by-design, and that portion of the finding is an enhancement suggestion rather than a production-readiness bug.
- The concrete, real defect is the failure-path correlation asymmetry (Claim B): run_end embeds experiment_run_id but run_failed does not, despite run_id being available and the run being marked FAILED. This is a true, deterministic, trivially-fixable inconsistency (add experiment_run_id=run_id to the run_failed payload, preserving hash-determinism just as run_end does) and the suggested fix is correct.

Net: the finding is confirmed (the described code behavior is real and the PoC reproduces), but its severity is low — the durability portion is intended design, and the only genuine bug (failure-path run_id omission) is a minor correlation gap with no security/data-loss/availability impact, affecting auditability of failed runs only.

confirmed.title: numpy resample-stability path allocates a full R×N boolean matrix (peak-memory blowup) and lacks an at-scale equivalence/memory test
confirmed.description: The numpy branch of _resample_stability computes null_arr[None,:] >= res_arr[:,None] (and the <= variant) before .sum(axis=1), materializing a dense R×N boolean array — O(R·N) PEAK MEMORY, whereas the pure-Python fallback it claims to merely speed up is O(N). Confirmed at confirm.py:216-217. At corpus scale (R=10k resamples vs N=10k permutation null = 10^8 booleans ≈ 100 MB transient; 20k×20k ≈ 400 MB) enabling the optional numpy extra silently raises peak memory by orders of magnitude and can OOM where the pure path would not, while the docstring frames it as 'purely a speed optimization, never a semantic change'. Compounding this, the only equivalence test (test_resample_stability_numpy_path_matches_pure_python, null=40/res=25) is far below any size where the mask is observable and pins no memory contract, so a future searchsorted/chunked rewrite has no at-scale guardrail.
confirmed.poc: null=list(rng.normal(size=20000)); res=list(rng.normal(0.5,size=20000)); confirm._resample_stability(res, null, 'greater', 0.05) transiently allocates ~400MB (a 20000x20000 bool mask), whereas the pure path's working set is O(N). No existing test exercises this size or any memory characteristic.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Sort the null once and use np.searchsorted for per-resample extreme counts in O((R+N)logN) time / O(R+N) memory: greater -> cnt = len(null) - searchsorted(null_sorted, res_arr, 'left'); less -> cnt = searchsorted(null_sorted, res_arr, 'right'); the (cnt+1)/(n+1)<=alpha test is byte-identical. If keeping the broadcast form, chunk the resamples and document the memory characteristic. Add a parametrized equivalence test at N=2000/R=2000 (and optionally a tracemalloc sub-quadratic peak-allocation regression test) so the rewrite is held to byte-identical results.

a.why:
Verified against current code. confirm.py:216-219 computes `(null_arr[None, :] >= res_arr[:, None]).sum(axis=1)` (and the `<=` variant for the 'less' tail), which materializes a dense R×N boolean array BEFORE the axis-1 reduction. numpy bool dtype is 1 byte/element, so R=N=20000 transiently allocates 4×10^8 bytes ≈ 400MB (10k×10k ≈ 100MB) — exactly as the finding states. The pure-Python fallback (confirm.py:223) streams one null pass per resample with an O(N) working set, so the numpy path is asymptotically worse in PEAK MEMORY, not merely faster. The docstring (lines 204-208) explicitly claims the numpy path is "purely a speed optimization for large R·N, never a semantic change," which is the precise asymmetry the finding flags: large R·N is where the blowup occurs.

Inputs are unbounded: resample_statistics and null_distribution are caller-supplied sequences with no size cap or chunking anywhere (confirm.py:310-315). The `baseline` optional extra installs numpy>=1.26 (pyproject.toml:68-69) and the prod extras bundle it (line 73), so this path is live in real deployments. The numpy import is try/except-guarded, so it only activates when the extra is present.

The only equivalence test (tests/test_confirm.py:191-208) runs at null=40/res=25 — far below any scale where the dense mask is observable — and pins no memory/peak-allocation contract, so a future searchsorted/chunked rewrite has no at-scale or memory guardrail. The suggested searchsorted fix is byte-identical: for 'greater', count(null >= res) = len(null) - searchsorted(null_sorted, res, 'left'); for 'less', count(null <= res) = searchsorted(null_sorted, res, 'right'); both feed the same (cnt+1)/(n+1) <= alpha test.

PoC could not be executed directly because numpy is not installed in this verification sandbox (python3 -c 'import numpy' -> ModuleNotFoundError), but the broadcast semantics and 1-byte bool dtype make the allocation deterministic and the memory arithmetic correct; the code path is unambiguous.

Severity is medium, not high: it requires BOTH the optional baseline extra AND corpus-scale R·N to manifest, the impact is a transient memory degradation / OOM risk rather than any correctness, semantic, or security defect (results remain identical), and the workaround (omit the extra) restores the O(N) path. It is a genuine production-readiness/scalability concern with a missing test contract, not speculative or intended design.

The numpy branch computes `(null_arr[None,:] >= res_arr[:,None]).sum(axis=1)` (and the `<=` variant), which materializes a dense R×N boolean array before reducing — O(R·N) peak memory, versus the O(N) working set of the pure-Python fallback at confirm.py:222-224. numpy 2.4.6 is installed in the project's .venv (Python 3.14.5), so this is the live execution path, not a theoretical/dead branch. confirmed.title: discover_and_confirm does not type-guard the confirmer's return value (asymmetric with adjudication/pipeline)
confirmed.description: In adjudicate_with_baseline (adjudication.py:253) and the pipeline runner (pipeline.py:563) a confirmer that forgets to return (yields None) is caught with an explicit isinstance(verdict, Verdict) guard (#F-021). The standalone discovery runner was left out: after verdict = confirm(hypothesis, held_out) (discovery.py:150) it goes straight to verdict.hypothesis_id (:151, which only checks id-mismatch, not type). A confirmer returning None raises an opaque AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'hypothesis_id' instead of the documented typed FirewallViolation. The module docstring (lines 94-97) promises FirewallViolation on a verdict<->hypothesis misattribution, and a None verdict never reaches that check, breaking the typed-exception contract for one of the two public Firewall-B entry points.
confirmed.poc: def bad_confirm(hyp, held_out): pass  # returns None. discover_and_confirm(corpus, split, discover=..., confirm=bad_confirm, authority=...) raises AttributeError("'NoneType' object has no attribute 'hypothesis_id'") instead of FirewallViolation('confirm returned NoneType ... expected a Verdict').

The finding cited pipeline.py:563 as a different path, but the guard exists there as described; only discovery.py lacks it, so the asymmetry across the public Firewall-B entry points is real. The module docstring (discovery.py:94-97) promises FirewallViolation on a verdict↔hypothesis misattribution, and a None verdict never reaches that typed check. Verdict is already imported at discovery.py:39, so the suggested one-line fix applies cleanly. PoC reproduced: a confirmer returning None (def bad_confirm(hyp, held_out): pass) makes discover_and_confirm raise `AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'hypothesis_id'` instead of the documented typed FirewallViolation. Severity is low: it only triggers on an author-supplied buggy confirmer; the process still halts loudly (no silent corruption, no firewall bypass). confirmed.title: from_records raises raw KeyError on a malformed record instead of the typed ProvenanceError
confirmed.description: from_records rebuilds ProvenanceEntry by directly subscripting each record (r['seq'], r['kind'], r['summary'], r['payload'], r['timestamp'], r['prev_hash'], r['entry_hash'] at provenance.py:266-275, confirmed). A record deserialized from an external/persistent store missing any key raises a bare KeyError, not the module's typed ProvenanceError. Every other deserialization/verification path here surfaces ProvenanceError (verify_records, _digest, clock/timestamp guards, freeze guard). The trail is explicitly a trust-boundary deserialization point ('rebuild entries ... and verify the chain'), so an externally-supplied record dropping/renaming a field should fail with the trail's typed error catchable alongside verify_records failures; a non-int seq / non-str field would also propagate untyped.
confirmed.poc: from_records([{ 'seq':0,'kind':'x','summary':'s','payload':{}, 'timestamp':'2020-01-01T00:00:00+00:00','prev_hash':'assay-provenance:v1:genesis' }]) -> KeyError: 'entry_hash' (expected ProvenanceError describing the malformed record).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Wrap the construction in try/except (KeyError, TypeError, ValueError) as exc: raise ProvenanceError(f'provenance record could not be rebuilt: {exc}') from None, then call verify_records on the typed-good entries.

a.why: Verified against current code. src/assay_engine/provenance.py:266-277 rebuilds ProvenanceEntry by direct subscript (r["seq"], r["kind"], r["summary"], r["payload"], r["timestamp"], r["prev_hash"], r["entry_hash"]) inside from_records, with no try/except. The PoC reproduces exactly: from_records([{seq,kind,summary,payload,timestamp,prev_hash}]) (missing entry_hash) raises raw KeyError('entry_hash'), not ProvenanceError (confirmed by running it under python3.14 with PYTHONPATH=src). from_records is an explicit deserialization/trust-boundary entry point ("Rebuild entries from ... and verify the chain"), and every other failure mode on this path (verify_records seq/linkage/hash mismatch at lines 248-256, _digest, clock/freeze guards) surfaces the module's typed ProvenanceError (defined line 49). A caller catching ProvenanceError to handle a corrupt/malformed external trail will not catch a dropped/renamed field; a non-int seq or non-str field would likewise propagate an untyped TypeError/ValueError through verify_records/_digest. The suggested fix (wrap construction in try/except (KeyError, TypeError, ValueError) -> raise ProvenanceError(...) from None, then verify_records) is correct. This is a real, reproducible, non-speculative concern, but it is an exception-type/consistency robustness gap at a trust boundary rather than a security bypass or integrity failure — chain integrity is still enforced by verify_records once entries are constructed; only structurally malformed records leak an untyped exception.

b.why: Verified against current code. src/assay_engine/provenance.py:266-277 rebuilds ProvenanceEntry by directly subscripting r["seq"], r["kind"], r["summary"], r["payload"], r["timestamp"], r["prev_hash"], r["entry_hash"] with no try/except, then calls verify_records. PoC reproduced exactly with the venv interpreter: from_records([...]) on a record missing entry_hash raises a bare `KeyError: 'entry_hash'`, NOT ProvenanceError. The module otherwise consistently surfaces the typed ProvenanceError(RuntimeError) at every other validation/deserialization point (provenance.py:85,119,131,133,138,143,156,176 and verify_records at 249,251,254). from_records is a documented trust-boundary deserialization entry point ("Rebuild entries from ProvenanceTrail.to_records output and verify the chain") consuming external/persisted records, so a dropped/renamed key (KeyError) or a wrong-typed value (TypeError/ValueError) leaks an untyped exception that callers cannot catch alongside verify_records failures, breaking the module's error-contract convention. Not speculative, not already-handled, not intended design. Severity is low: it is an error-typing/robustness defect at a deserialization boundary, not an integrity/security defect — the HMAC chain cannot be forged because verify_records still rejects any entry that does successfully construct. Suggested fix (wrap construction in try/except (KeyError, TypeError, ValueError) -> raise ProvenanceError(...) from None) is appropriate.

confirmed.title: test_install_flush_on_exit_wires_atexit_and_sigterm passes even if the SIGTERM handler wiring (#O3) is fully reverted
confirmed.description: The test asserts BOTH atexit and the SIGTERM handler are wired, but the SIGTERM half is asserted only inside `if "h" in captured:` (tests/test_observability.py:188-190). captured['h'] is populated solely as a side effect of the source calling signal.signal(SIGTERM, _handler) (tracing.py:141). If a developer reverts the SIGTERM block (tracing.py:131-141) keeping only atexit.register (:130), signal.signal is never invoked, captured stays empty, the if-branch is skipped, and the test passes on the atexit assertions alone. The SIGTERM portion of this regression guard is vacuous on the exact revert it is meant to catch — the conditional turns a should-be-required assertion into a no-op when the fix is missing.
confirmed.poc: Delete the `if threading.current_thread() is threading.main_thread(): ... signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, _handler)` block (tracing.py:131-141), keep atexit.register(lambda: _safe(flush)). The test still passes: atexit handler registered and flush>=1; captured is empty so the SIGTERM assertions are skipped.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Since pytest runs the body on the main thread, unconditionally assert 'h' in captured and exercise it: captured['h'](signal.SIGTERM, None); assert calls['shutdown'] >= 1. Remove the if-guard so a reverted handler fails the test.

a.why: Verified against current code at /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay (source is src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py, not src/hc_assay/... as the finding stated, but the line numbers and content match exactly). tests/test_observability.py:188 wraps the SIGTERM assertions (lines 189-190) in `if "h" in captured:`. captured['h'] is set only as a side effect of the source calling signal.signal at tracing.py:141 inside the main-thread block (tracing.py:131-143). I reproduced the PoC: removed the entire `if threading.current_thread() is threading.main_thread(): ... signal.signal(...)` block while keeping atexit.register(lambda: _safe(flush)) at :130, then ran the test with the project venv pytest — it PASSED (1 passed). With the SIGTERM block reverted, signal.signal is never called, captured stays empty, the if-branch is skipped, and the test passes on the atexit assertions alone. Restored source; git diff clean; test still passes normally. Because pytest executes the test body on the main thread, signal.signal is ALWAYS invoked in correct code, so the conditional has no legitimate purpose and only masks the exact regression it should catch — the SIGTERM half of this guard is vacuous on revert. The suggested fix (unconditionally assert 'h' in captured, invoke captured['h'](SIGTERM, None), assert calls['shutdown'] >= 1) is correct. This is a genuine, non-speculative regression-guard defect. The production #O3 code itself is correct; the concern is that a future revert losing SIGTERM/container-stop span flush would not be caught by CI. That makes it a real but indirect production-readiness issue, hence medium severity rather than high.

b.why:
VERIFIED against current code at /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay (note: source path is src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py, not src/hc_assay/... as the finding's prose header implies, but the content matches exactly).

Code facts confirmed:
- tests/test_observability.py:188-190 asserts the SIGTERM half ONLY inside `if "h" in captured:`. captured['h'] is set solely by the monkeypatched signal.signal (line 180), which the source invokes at tracing.py:141.
- tracing.py:130 = atexit.register(lambda: _safe(flush)); tracing.py:131-143 = the main-thread-guarded SIGTERM block that calls signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, _handler). The handler calls force_flush + shutdown (#O3).

With the original code restored (git diff --stat clean) the test also passes.
