confirmed.title: Comment claims the determinism freeze keeps an array 'opaque/O(1)' but freeze() copies the whole buffer via tobytes() (O(N))
confirmed.description: The docstring of cosine_similarity_matrix_array() (primitives.py:125) states 'the determinism freeze keeps an array opaque/O(1), unlike a nested list'. The actual freeze() path for an array-like (_frozen.py ~109-118) calls value.tobytes() and wraps it in a (kind, shape, bytes) descriptor, re-freezing that. tobytes() materializes/copies every byte — O(N) in elements, not O(1). The intended contrast is real (one tobytes() call rather than per-element Python recursion) but the literal 'O(1)' claim is false; both memory and time for freezing an array are O(N).
confirmed.poc: For an ndarray of n elements freeze() executes tobytes(), allocating/copying n*itemsize bytes — linear in n; a 10M-element array produces a 10M*itemsize-byte bytes object each freeze. 'O(1)' is therefore false.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Soften the comment to what is true: the freeze traverses an array in a single tobytes() pass (O(1) traversal depth / no per-element Python recursion) but the conversion is O(N) in array size. Replace 'opaque/O(1)' with 'opaque, frozen in a single tobytes() pass (no per-element recursion), unlike a nested list'.

a.why: Verified against current code. primitives.py:125 docstring of cosine_similarity_matrix_array() states "the determinism freeze keeps an array opaque/O(1), unlike a nested list". The freeze path is in src/assay_engine/_frozen.py (not baseline/_frozen.py as the finding's path hint said, but the code is present): lines 109-118 detect an array-like with tobytes() and at line 118 return freeze((str(kind), tuple(shape)..., tobytes())). value.tobytes() materializes/copies the entire buffer — O(N) in element count and itemsize for both time and memory. The PoC is reproducible: a 10M-element ndarray yields a 10M*itemsize-byte bytes object on every freeze. The literal "O(1)" claim is therefore false. The intended contrast (a single tobytes() pass with no per-element Python recursion, vs nested-list per-element freeze recursion) is real, but the "O(1)" wording is wrong. Confirmed as a true documentation-accuracy defect in current code. Severity is low: it is solely a misleading docstring comment; the runtime behavior is correct, there is no functional/correctness/security/performance regression introduced by the comment, and the array path is not actually claimed to be unused at scale incorrectly. It is a genuine but minor concern, well addressed by the suggested softening of the wording.

b.why: Verified in current code. The docstring at /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/baseline/primitives.py:125 states "the determinism freeze keeps an array opaque/O(1), unlike a nested list". The freeze path is at /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/_frozen.py:109-118 (note: the finding cited _frozen.py under baseline/, but the actual file is at src/assay_engine/_frozen.py). At line 118 freeze() canonicalizes an array-like by calling tobytes(): `return freeze((str(kind), tuple(shape)..., tobytes()))`. tobytes() materializes/copies the entire buffer (n*itemsize bytes), which is O(N) in both time and memory, not O(1). The PoC is reproducible: a 10M-element ndarray yields a 10M*itemsize-byte bytes object on each freeze. The literal "O(1)" claim is therefore false. The intended contrast (single tobytes pass vs per-element Python recursion for a nested list) is real and is the genuine engineering benefit, but the asymptotic claim as written is incorrect. This is a documentation-accuracy defect only: no runtime, correctness, security, or data-integrity impact — the freeze produces a correct, hashable, deterministic descriptor regardless. Severity is low (misleading comment that could lead a builder to wrongly assume freezing a large array is free).

rejected.title: _CONN_INIT_LOCKS and _POOLS_BY_CONN grow unboundedly — never pruned, keyed by raw conn_str
rejected.description: _CONN_INIT_LOCKS is a process-lifetime dict keyed by resolved conn_str with add-only lifecycle (never removed); _POOLS_BY_CONN is the same. conn_str distinctness includes any URL variation (host alias, optional params, credential rotation that changes userinfo, sslmode toggles). A long-lived process targeting many distinct DSNs (multi-tenant runner, test harness rotating DSNs, credential rotation) accumulates a Lock per distinct string and, for _POOLS_BY_CONN, a live ConnectionPool + worker thread per distinct string with no eviction — an unbounded cache and thread/connection leak. Credential rotation makes the SAME logical DB appear as a NEW conn_str, orphaning the old pool (atexit-closed but never reused/closed until exit).
rejected.poc: Loop setting ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL to N distinct valid loopback DSNs (e.g. differing in ?application_name=run_i) and call get_checkpointer() each iteration; len(_CONN_INIT_LOCKS) and len(_POOLS_BY_CONN) grow to N and worker-thread count climbs by one per distinct DSN, none reclaimed.
rejected.suggested_fix: Canonicalize the cache key (drop volatile query params / strip userinfo for the key while keeping it for connect) so cosmetic/credential changes map to one entry; cap _POOLS_BY_CONN with an LRU that pool.close()es and removes the evicted pool from _OPEN_POOLS; prune _CONN_INIT_LOCKS for keys no longer in _POOLS_BY_CONN.

a.why:
The mechanical facts are all accurate against current code. In src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py: _CONN_INIT_LOCKS (line 85) is created add-only in _conn_init_lock() (lines 188-194, never removed); _POOLS_BY_CONN (line 89) is written at line 298 and never evicted; the cache key is the raw conn_str from get_postgres_connection_string() (line 238), which only validates loopback via require_local_uri (src/assay_engine/_local.py — returns the URL unchanged, no canonicalization). Each distinct conn_str opens a live ConnectionPool with open=True + a worker thread (lines 266-278), registered in _OPEN_POOLS, closed only at atexit (line 184). The test at tests/test_persistence.py:460-473 confirms distinct conn_strs each get their own pool. So the PoC (loop N distinct DSNs) is reproducible and the structures grow to N with one pool/thread each, none reclaimed.

However this is NOT a genuine production-readiness concern in the current code; it is a speculative hardening nicety. (1) Scope: ADR-0003 (docs/decisions/ADR-0003-local-first-data-sovereign.md) defines a single-box, data-sovereign, single-operator deployment. ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL is a static env var, in practice set once per process; there is no multi-tenant runner (explicitly out of scope) and no code path that injects per-run query params like ?application_name=run_i. (2) The one-pool-per-distinct-conn_str design is the INTENDED, already-reviewed bound: the prior real issue (per-call pool leak, PASS-2 lines 294-302, confirmed LOW) was fixed by #144 precisely by keying a cache on conn_str, with the accepted invariant being 'bounds threads/connections at one pool per distinct conn_str.' The number of distinct conn_strs is the implicit bound and is effectively 1 in the documented deployment. (3) No in-scope caller varies the DSN within a live process — grep shows the only production callers go through configured_checkpointer with a static env. The triggering scenarios (multi-tenant, DSN-rotating harness, live credential rotation re-reading a changed os.environ) all contradict the documented single-DSN model; credential rotation in this architecture restarts the process. Unlike the PASS-2 finding which was triggerable by an in-scope per-study-run call pattern, G-008's leak requires usage the architecture explicitly excludes. The suggested LRU/canonicalization is reasonable defense-in-depth but not a production-readiness defect in the current code.

b.why:
The structural code facts in the finding are accurate: in src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py, _CONN_INIT_LOCKS (line 85, populated at 191-193) and _POOLS_BY_CONN (line 89, populated at 298) are process-lifetime, add-only dicts keyed by the raw resolved conn_str with NO canonicalization, NO LRU cap, and NO eviction. conn_str comes verbatim from get_postgres_connection_string() (line 154, ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL env) so cosmetic variants (?application_name=..., sslmode toggles, rotated userinfo) are distinct keys. Each distinct key builds a new psycopg_pool.ConnectionPool (its own background worker thread, lines 266-278), registered in _OPEN_POOLS and closed only at atexit (179-185, 168-176). The PoC is mechanically reproducible exactly as written.

However, it is NOT a genuine production-readiness problem in the CURRENT code for THIS product, and the triggering scenarios are speculative against the actual deployment model:

1. Every conn_str is forced through require_local_uri (line 155 -> src/assay_engine/_local.py:90), which rejects any non-loopback host. There is no multi-tenant/remote fan-out capability.
2. There is no multi-tenant model anywhere (grep for "tenant" in src returns nothing) and NO runtime code path that mutates ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL — only tests monkeypatch it. No caller in src outside the persistence package even invokes get_checkpointer/configured_checkpointer.
3. The documented and enforced deployment model (ADR-0003, module docstring lines 4-6, _DEFAULT_PG line 48 "postgresql://localhost:5432/assay") is a single on-box logical store. In normal operation conn_str is constant, so both dicts hold exactly ONE entry — that single-entry cache is the entire purpose of the #144 fast path (lines 240-245).

The "multi-tenant runner", "test harness rotating DSNs", and "credential rotation" triggers cited by the finding are not real code paths in this repo; they are hypothetical. Even under rare loopback credential rotation, growth is a handful of entries over a long process life with orphaned pools atexit-closed — not an operational leak. The "unbounded" growth is unbounded only in theory; in practice it is bounded to ~1. This is at most a low-value defense-in-depth/hardening nicety (canonicalizing the key, adding an LRU), not a production defect. Per the instruction to confirm only genuine, non-speculative concerns in current code, this does not meet the bar.

all.title: Post-hoc direction selection (HARKing) for whole-corpus hypotheses locked with predicted_direction=None
all.description: _resolve_direction accepts a confirm-time predicted_direction whenever the locked hypothesis has predicted_direction=None, and nothing forces a WHOLE_CORPUS hypothesis to lock a non-None direction. A study can pre-register a directionless whole-corpus hypothesis, inspect the observed statistic/baseline, then choose 'greater' or 'less' at confirm_whole_corpus time to land in whichever tail is significant — textbook HARKing that defeats the pre-registration binding (issue #24 the docstring claims to close). Verified in source: when locked_dir is None and predicted_direction is None it raises, but when predicted_direction is supplied it is used as the resolved tail, so the verdict polarity is chosen post-hoc on a hypothesis whose cryptographic lock binds 'no direction'.
all.poc: Lock a WHOLE_CORPUS Hypothesis with predicted_direction=None via lock_hypothesis (proof binds None). After observing obs=95.0 against a 0..99 null, call confirm_whole_corpus(..., predicted_direction='greater', authority=auth) -> 'supported'; had obs been low, pass 'less' instead. Pre-registration verification passes; tail chosen after seeing data.
all.suggested_fix: Require predicted_direction at lock time for WHOLE_CORPUS hypotheses (raise in Hypothesis.__post_init__ when kind is WHOLE_CORPUS and direction is None), or in _resolve_direction reject locked_dir is None for any hypothesis going through require_preregistered (accept a confirm-time direction only for unlocked exploratory hypotheses). The directionless-deferred path must be unreachable for a pre-registered confirmatory test.

a.why:
Confirmed as a true defect in current code (3 passes applied). Trace:

1. Hypothesis.__post_init__ (src/assay_engine/methodology/hypothesis.py:80-97) explicitly permits predicted_direction=None for ANY kind including WHOLE_CORPUS. The inline comment at lines 86-87 states None is "deferred to confirm time on the whole-corpus path" — i.e. the directionless-deferred path is intentional and reachable, with no guard requiring WHOLE_CORPUS to lock a non-None direction.

2. canonical_hypothesis_digest (preregistration.py:109-119) includes predicted_direction in the digest. Locking a None-direction hypothesis binds "None" into the proof; verify_preregistration (preregistration.py:222-257) only checks the proof matches the CURRENT content (still None), so it passes. The cryptographic binding is satisfied precisely because no direction was committed.

3. confirm_whole_corpus (confirm.py:289-303) runs _gate_preregistration (which only verifies the lock/timestamp) then calls _resolve_direction.

4. _resolve_direction (confirm.py:238-252): when locked_dir is None and a confirm-time predicted_direction is supplied, it returns the confirm-time value (line 252) with NO re-validation against the lock. The verdict polarity (p_support vs p_contradict / supported vs contradicted, confirm.py:304-337) is then computed from a tail chosen after observing the data.

PoC is reproducible exactly as described: lock a WHOLE_CORPUS hypothesis with predicted_direction=None, observe obs=95 vs a 0..99 null, pass predicted_direction='greater' -> 'supported'; had obs been low, pass 'less'. All pre-registration gates pass. This is textbook HARKing/post-hoc tail selection.

This directly contradicts the engine's stated guarantee: docstrings at confirm.py:231 ("Direction is fixed at lock time on the hypothesis (issue #24)") and confirm.py:277 ("The predicted tail is taken from the hypothesis's pre-registered predicted_direction") are FALSE for the None-locked path. The methodology engine exists to prevent exactly this; the deferred-None path is a sanctioned escape hatch that defeats the pre-registration binding for confirmatory whole-corpus tests.

Severity high rather than critical: it requires the study author to deliberately lock a directionless hypothesis (not a silent/default-triggered bug), and the suggested fix is straightforward (require predicted_direction at lock time for WHOLE_CORPUS in __post_init__, or in _resolve_direction reject locked_dir is None for any pre-registered/gated confirmation). But it is a genuine, non-speculative integrity hole in the core scientific-validity contract, so it warrants more than medium.

b.why:
Confirmed as a true, current, reproducible methodology-integrity defect (HARKing) in the post-3-pass code.

Mechanism verified in source:
- src/assay_engine/methodology/hypothesis.py:80-97 — Hypothesis.__post_init__ permits predicted_direction=None for ANY kind, including WHOLE_CORPUS. The inline comment at lines 86-87 explicitly endorses "None (deferred to confirm time on the whole-corpus path)". There is no guard requiring a non-None direction for WHOLE_CORPUS.
- src/assay_engine/methodology/preregistration.py:117 — canonical_hypothesis_digest binds predicted_direction into the proof, so locking with None cryptographically attests "no direction"; the proof still verifies later when a confirm-time direction is supplied.
- src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:230-258 — _resolve_direction: when locked_dir is None (line 238-239 false), it only raises if the confirm-time predicted_direction is ALSO None (line 246-247); otherwise the else branch (line 251-252) accepts the confirm-time direction as the resolved tail. confirm_whole_corpus (line 303) then uses this for the verdict.

PoC reproduced (run with the FULL pre-registration gate via authority=auth, i.e. require_preregistered content-binding + timestamp + ordering, NOT the weak presence gate):
  h = WHOLE_CORPUS, predicted_direction=None; locked via lock_hypothesis (proof binds None and verifies).
  null = 0..99.
  confirm_whole_corpus(observed=200, resamples=[200]*50, predicted_direction='greater', authority=auth) -> label 'supported'.
  confirm_whole_corpus(observed=-100, resamples=[-100]*50, predicted_direction='less', authority=auth) -> label 'supported'.
The identical locked, proof-verified hypothesis yields 'supported' in EITHER tail purely by choosing the confirm-time direction after observing the data — textbook HARKing that defeats the pre-registration binding the confirm.py:231 docstring claims to close (issue #24). (An earlier run with non-extreme obs returned 'indeterminate' only because the resample-stability threshold wasn't met; with a reproducible extreme effect it lands 'supported' in whichever tail the analyst picks.)

This is not speculative, not already-handled, and contradicts the module's own stated guarantee ("Direction is fixed at lock time ... so it cannot be chosen post-hoc"). The directionless-deferred path is reachable for a fully pre-registered confirmatory test, so the pre-registration binding is bypassable. Suggested fix in the finding is sound: forbid predicted_direction=None for WHOLE_CORPUS at construction/lock, or make _resolve_direction reject locked_dir is None for any pre-registered (require_preregistered) hypothesis, accepting a confirm-time direction only for unlocked exploratory use.

Severity: high — it is a methodology-correctness/integrity defect in the core confirmatory engine of a tool whose entire value proposition is rigorous, tamper-evident pre-registration; it silently converts a directionless pre-registration into a guaranteed-significant verdict. It is not 'critical' in the code-safety/RCE sense and requires the study author to author a directionless WHOLE_CORPUS hypothesis, but it directly undermines the headline scientific guarantee.

all.title: Bootstrap lock_timeout session-GUC leaks into pooled connection used for all later checkpoint writes
all.description: _acquire_migration_lock runs SELECT set_config('lock_timeout', %s, false) — is_local=false, i.e. SESSION scope (confirmed at source line ~210). The connection comes from `with pool.connection() as conn:` and is returned to the same pool on block exit; psycopg_pool does not reset session state without a reset= callback (none configured). That pool is then used by PostgresSaver for every subsequent checkpoint read/write, so the bootstrap-only lock_timeout (default 30s) silently persists on a long-lived pooled connection. Under lock contention a normal checkpoint write that would otherwise block briefly aborts with 'canceling statement due to lock_timeout' — durability loss at exactly the interrupt/resume the checkpointer exists to guarantee.
all.poc: With ASSAY_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS=30000, run get_checkpointer() to bootstrap, then re-check-out a connection and assert conn.execute('SHOW lock_timeout').fetchone() != '0'. Two concurrent resumes contending on the same checkpoint row produce a spurious 'canceling statement due to lock_timeout' instead of completing.
all.suggested_fix: Reset the GUC on the same connection before block exit after setup()/advisory-unlock (conn.execute("SELECT set_config('lock_timeout','0',false)") or RESET lock_timeout in finally); or configure ConnectionPool(reset=lambda c: c.execute('RESET ALL')); or run bootstrap on a dedicated one-shot psycopg.connect() that is closed rather than returned to the pool.

a.why: Confirmed in current code. checkpoint.py:212 runs `SELECT set_config('lock_timeout', %s, false)` with is_local=false (SESSION scope), not transaction-local. The connection is obtained via `with pool.connection() as conn:` (line 283) from a ConnectionPool constructed at lines 266-278 with NO `reset=` callback. psycopg_pool's default connection reset does not issue RESET ALL — with autocommit=True there is no transaction to roll back and no reset callback, so the SESSION-scoped lock_timeout persists when the connection returns to the pool. That same pool is wrapped in PostgresSaver at line 299 and used for all subsequent checkpoint reads/writes. Default ASSAY_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS=30000 (line 61) makes the `if timeout_ms:` guard (line 211) true by default, so the leak occurs in the default configuration. With default min_size=1 (line 263) the bootstrap connection IS the long-lived pooled connection serving subsequent traffic. Under lock contention a checkpoint write that would normally block briefly aborts with 'canceling statement due to lock_timeout' — durability loss at the interrupt/resume the checkpointer exists to guarantee, contradicting the module's own stated invariant (docstring lines 13-17). The advisory lock auto-releases on connection return, but lock_timeout does not. PoC (bootstrap, re-checkout, SHOW lock_timeout != '0') is reproducible given a live Postgres. Not speculative, not already-handled, not intended design. Could not execute the PoC directly because psycopg_pool (the optional 'persistence' extra) is not installed in this environment; verification rests on the source code and documented psycopg_pool reset semantics. Rated high rather than critical because it surfaces only under lock contention, but it directly undermines the durability guarantee. Valid fix options: RESET lock_timeout in the finally block on the same connection, configure ConnectionPool(reset=...), or bootstrap on a one-shot psycopg.connect() that is closed.

b.why: Verified against current code at /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py. Line 212: conn.execute("SELECT set_config('lock_timeout', %s, false)", ...) sets lock_timeout with is_local=false (SESSION scope). The connection is autocommit=True (line 271), so it cannot be transaction-scoped anyway. The connection is obtained via `with pool.connection() as conn:` (line 283) and returns to the pool on block exit. The ConnectionPool constructor (lines 266-278) configures no reset= callback; psycopg_pool's default putconn behavior rolls back open transactions but does NOT RESET session GUCs, so lock_timeout persists. The finally block (lines 290-296) only runs pg_advisory_unlock — it does not reset lock_timeout. That same pool is then cached (line 298) and wrapped in PostgresSaver for every subsequent checkpoint read/write (lines 245, 254, 299). With the default min_size=1 (line 263), the leaked connection is the single persistent pooled connection, making reproduction reliable. A grep confirms no lock_timeout reset and no reset= callback anywhere in src/ or tests/; no test covers cleanup of the GUC (tests only assert it is SET, lines 481/677). Consequence matches description: a contended checkpoint write aborts with 'canceling statement due to lock_timeout' instead of waiting — durability loss at the exact interrupt/resume the checkpointer exists to guarantee (the failure the module docstring lines 13-17 claims to prevent). This is genuine, not speculative, not already-handled, not intended design. Severity is high rather than critical because: lock_timeout is only triggered under actual row-lock contention (e.g., two concurrent resumes on the same checkpoint), it is a non-default code path (requires ASSAY_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS != 0, though 30000 is the default), and it degrades but does not silently corrupt — the failed write raises rather than persisting bad state.

all.title: BULK tier has no inner cancellation (asymmetric with HIGH_STAKES) — a hung backend permanently leaks a pool slot and drives endless retryable backpressure
all.description: Confirmed in source: HIGH_STAKES wraps its stream in `anyio.fail_after(HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT)` (seam.py:438) so a hung subprocess is cancelled inside the worker and the bounded-pool slot is freed. BULK has no equivalent — it relies only on client_kwargs={'timeout': BULK_TIMEOUT} reaching httpx (seam.py:349) and the outer _with_timeout(..., BULK_TIMEOUT+10) (seam.py:510), which by its own docstring cannot interrupt a thread blocked in a backend call. If the httpx timeout does not fire (wedged stream, proxy holding the socket, ChatOllama not propagating client_kwargs, C-level blocking read) the worker is stuck forever and its _inflight slot never releases. _submit_bounded reports saturation as a RETRYABLE RateLimitError ('pool saturated', seam.py:223) and _run_with_retries treats it as transient backpressure, so once enough BULK workers wedge (up to _POOL_WORKERS=8) every future call livelocks on saturation backpressure that never clears.
all.poc: Monkeypatch _bulk_complete to block with no timeout (threading.Event().wait()). Submit _POOL_WORKERS BULK requests via _with_timeout; each returns FuturesTimeout to the caller but the worker stays alive holding its slot. After 8, every _submit_bounded raises RateLimitError('saturated') indefinitely; slots never recover for the rest of process life.
all.suggested_fix: Give BULK the same in-worker hard bound as HIGH_STAKES (run the generation under anyio.fail_after(BULK_TIMEOUT) via an async ollama client, or a cancellable subprocess). At minimum add a watchdog that surfaces a PermanentReasoningError ('reasoning pool wedged') on saturation that never clears rather than infinite RateLimitError backpressure, and assert ChatOllama honors client_kwargs timeout on the streaming path.

a.why: Verified against current code at /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py. The asymmetry is real: HIGH_STAKES wraps its stream in `with anyio.fail_after(HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT)` (seam.py:438) inside `_drain`, which truly cancels query() and tears down the child subprocess, freeing the pool slot. BULK's `_bulk_complete` (319-383) calls `client.invoke(messages)` synchronously at line 363 with NO cancellation wrapper — it relies only on `client_kwargs={"timeout": BULK_TIMEOUT}` (349) and the outer `_with_timeout(..., BULK_TIMEOUT+10)` (510). The outer `_with_timeout` provably cannot interrupt a wedged worker: its own comment at seam.py:259-261 states "a ThreadPoolExecutor cannot interrupt a thread already blocked in a backend call. The worker keeps its slot until the call returns." So if the httpx timeout fails to fire (wedged stream, proxy holding the socket, ChatOllama not propagating client_kwargs, or a C-level blocking read), the BULK slot leaks permanently. `_submit_bounded` (218-226) raises a RETRYABLE RateLimitError once `_inflight >= _POOL_WORKERS=8`. The maintainers themselves acknowledge this exact residual risk in the BULK_NUM_PREDICT comment (seam.py:95-98): "the wall-clock timeout cannot interrupt a call already blocked in the backend (a leaked pool slot)." BULK_NUM_PREDICT guards runaway *generation length* but not a socket/C-level wedge. The PoC (monkeypatch _bulk_complete to block on threading.Event().wait(), submit 8 BULK requests) is reproducible: each caller gets a FuturesTimeout while the worker stays alive holding its slot, and after 8 every subsequent submit hits the saturation guard, with slots never recovering for process life.\n\nTwo inaccuracies in the finding's framing that do NOT negate it: (1) the finding calls the backpressure "infinite"/"livelock indefinitely." It is not infinite — `_run_with_retries` caps rate-limit retries at RATE_LIMIT_MAX_RETRIES (default 5) at seam.py:575-576 and re-raises, and RUN_JSON_DEADLINE/deadline bound wall-clock. The accurate description is a PERMANENT process-wide reasoning outage surfaced as a RateLimitError on every call (after 5 bounded retries), with slots never recovering — which matches the finding's own end-state claim ("slots never recover for the rest of process life"). (2) The trigger requires the precondition that the httpx timeout does not fire; in the common case it does. But that precondition is realistic for a local Ollama runtime under crash/load, and the whole point of HIGH_STAKES's inner fail_after is that the maintainers already deemed this class of leak worth a hard in-worker bound — leaving BULK without the equivalent is a genuine, unintended asymmetry, not intended design. The suggested fix (give BULK an in-worker hard bound equivalent to HIGH_STAKES, e.g. run generation under anyio.fail_after via an async client, or surface PermanentReasoningError on never-clearing saturation) is correct and addresses the root cause.

b.why:
TRUE production-readiness defect, independently confirmed by reading current code and a reproduced PoC.

Asymmetry is real (cited): HIGH_STAKES wraps its stream in `with anyio.fail_after(HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT): await _drain_stream()` (seam.py:438) — a hard in-worker cancellation added in pass 2 (#127) precisely to free a leaked pool slot. BULK (`_bulk_complete`, seam.py:319-383) has NO equivalent: its synchronous `client.invoke(messages)` (seam.py:363) is protected only by `client_kwargs={"timeout": BULK_TIMEOUT}` (seam.py:349) and the outer `_with_timeout(..., BULK_TIMEOUT+10)` (seam.py:510). `_with_timeout`'s own docstring (seam.py:258-262) states a ThreadPoolExecutor cannot interrupt a thread blocked in a backend call and that it does NOT call future.cancel().

The unbounded path is concrete, not speculative. In the installed/pinned stack (langchain-ollama 1.1.0, ollama 0.6.2, httpx 0.28.1): client_kwargs DOES propagate (ChatOllama line 956 `Client(host=..., **sync_client_kwargs)` -> ollama BaseClient `httpx.Client(timeout=timeout)` at _client.py:112-115), so the finding's "ChatOllama not propagating client_kwargs" sub-hypothesis is actually false here. BUT the deeper problem holds: ChatOllama's `invoke()`/`_generate` forces `"stream": kwargs.pop("stream", True)` (chat_models.py:800), so even a non-streaming call goes through `self._client.chat(stream=True)` -> ollama `_request` uses `self._client.stream(...)` + `r.iter_lines()` (_client.py:183-190). httpx applies the scalar timeout as a PER-READ `read` timeout (verified: httpx.Timeout(120) -> read=120 applied per body read), NOT a total-stream wall clock. A wedged/slow-dribbling backend or interposed proxy that emits any byte within each BULK_TIMEOUT window keeps the worker alive indefinitely; `num_predict=2048` bounds model tokens but not non-token bytes (keepalive/whitespace/proxy chunking). There is no inner cancellation, so the worker outlives `BULK_TIMEOUT+10` and the slot is never released.

PoC reproduced (monkeypatched `_bulk_complete` to block, as the finding specifies): filling all _POOL_WORKERS=8 slots pins `_inflight=8/8` permanently; an unrelated healthy BULK call then fails with `RateLimitError: reasoning timeout pool saturated (8/8 in-flight)` after exhausting the rate-limit budget; `_inflight` stays at 8 (permanent leak, recovery only by restart). _submit_bounded (seam.py:223) classifies saturation as a RETRYABLE RateLimitError and _run_with_retries (seam.py:574-578) treats it as transient backpressure, so a permanently-wedged pool is indistinguishable from transient pressure — every subsequent call of any tier degrades, with no PermanentReasoningError('pool wedged') signal. This is the residual half of the exact defect PASS-2 confirmed as HIGH and fixed for HIGH_STAKES.

Severity HIGH (not critical): it requires a degraded dependency (wedged/dribbling backend or proxy) rather than firing under normal operation, and each individual caller still receives an error rather than blocking forever — but once triggered the shared module-global pool is permanently and process-wide degraded across all studies/tiers with no recovery short of restart. The suggested fix (give BULK an in-worker hard bound, or at minimum surface a PermanentReasoningError when saturation never clears, plus assert the streaming timeout) is the right shape.

all.title: unfreeze() promises 'stable order for reproducible output' for frozensets but the unorderable-element fallback iterates in non-deterministic order
all.description: The unfreeze() docstring (line 146) states 'frozenset -> sorted list (stable order for reproducible output)' and the inline comment (line ~153) claims a 'stable repr'. Confirmed: the implementation tries sorted(value) and on TypeError falls back to [unfreeze(v) for v in value], iterating the frozenset directly. Frozenset iteration order for str elements varies with PYTHONHASHSEED and for FrozenDict/object elements with id-based hashing varies per run, so output order is NOT reproducible — contradicting both the docstring and the comment (the F-032 class). unfreeze() is the documented thaw for public payloads (GateReview.payload, #141) and determinism/provenance records that exist to be byte-for-byte reproducible, so non-deterministic ordering can break serialized-artifact comparison.
all.poc: frozenset(['alpha',1,'beta',2,'gamma']) hits the fallback (mixed str/int unorderable); across PYTHONHASHSEED=0/1/2 unfreeze returns three different orderings of the same input. A frozenset of FrozenDicts (sorted raises TypeError) is similarly non-deterministic.
all.suggested_fix: Make the fallback deterministic: return [unfreeze(v) for v in sorted(value, key=repr)] (or key=canonical_json(unfreeze(v)) for a fully type-faithful, process-stable key) and update the comment to say it sorts by a process-stable key.

a.why: Verified against current code at /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/_frozen.py. The docstring (line 146) states "frozenset -> sorted list (stable order for reproducible output)" and the inline comment (line 154) claims a "stable repr". The implementation at lines 153-158 tries sorted(value) and, on TypeError, falls back to [unfreeze(v) for v in value], iterating the frozenset directly. PoC reproduced with the project's venv python: frozenset(['alpha',1,'beta',2,'gamma']) hits the fallback (str/int unorderable) and returns three different orderings across PYTHONHASHSEED=0/1/2: [1,'alpha',2,'beta','gamma'], [1,2,'alpha','beta','gamma'], [1,2,'gamma','alpha','beta']. A frozenset of FrozenDicts is likewise non-deterministic ([{'b':2},{'a':1},{'c':3}] vs [{'c':3},{'a':1},{'b':2}]). The path is real: freeze() converts any set to frozenset (_frozen.py:102), and unfreeze() is the documented thaw used in serialization paths that exist to be reproducible — GateReview.payload (pipeline.py:107) and provenance export (provenance.py:227). Output order is therefore NOT reproducible, contradicting both the docstring and the comment. Not speculative, not already-handled. Suggested fix (sorted(value, key=repr)) is sound and yields process-stable order. Severity is medium rather than high because the defect only triggers for sets whose elements are mutually unorderable (mixed-type, or sets-of-mappings/objects); the common homogeneous str/int set takes the sorted() branch and is already deterministic.

b.why: Verified against current code. src/assay_engine/_frozen.py:146 docstring promises 'frozenset -> sorted list (stable order for reproducible output)' and the inline comment at line 154 claims a 'stable repr'. The implementation (lines 153-158) does sorted(value) when elements are orderable, but on TypeError falls back to `[unfreeze(v) for v in value]` (line 158), iterating the frozenset directly with no deterministic key. PoC reproduced with the project's interpreter (.venv/bin/python 3.14.5): frozenset(['alpha',1,'beta',2,'gamma']) hits the fallback (mixed str/int is unorderable -> sorted raises TypeError) and returns three different orderings across PYTHONHASHSEED=0/1/2 ([1,'alpha',2,'beta','gamma'] / [1,2,'alpha','beta','gamma'] / [1,2,'gamma','alpha','beta']). A frozenset of FrozenDicts (sorted raises TypeError; hashing is id-based) likewise reordered across seeds. This contradicts both the docstring and the comment. unfreeze() is the documented thaw for determinism/provenance records (src/assay_engine/provenance.py:227 entries_to_records, designed to be byte-for-byte re-checkable via verify_records) and for public GateReview.payload (src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:107), so non-deterministic ordering can break serialized-artifact comparison. The suggested one-line fix (sorted(value, key=repr)) is correct and the contract is explicitly reproducibility, so this is a genuine concern, not intended design. Severity tempered to medium: the str-element trigger requires randomized PYTHONHASHSEED (CPython does not randomize by default unless set) AND a frozenset of mutually-unorderable elements; homogeneous frozensets (the common provenance case) take the deterministic sorted() path. The id-hashed-object case is non-deterministic every run regardless of seed, but such payloads are not JSON-serializable leaves and are an edge case. all.title: Contradicted verdict reported without any stability requirement, asymmetric with supported
all.description: In confirm_whole_corpus a 'supported' verdict requires significance in the predicted tail AND stability >= threshold across resamples, but a 'contradicted' verdict is returned as soon as p_contra <= alpha, before stability is consulted, and stability is only ever measured in the predicted (support) tail. A single noisy observed statistic landing in the opposite tail yields a hard 'contradicted' with no reproducibility check, while the symmetric 'supported' claim is gated. In the §5 source-scoring path this means contradictions of external claims can be driven by non-reproducible noise, biasing alignment_rate downward (every unstable opposite-tail blip increments n_contradicted in SourceScorecard). The docstring frames this as intentional but it is a real methodological inconsistency: reproduction is demanded for one verdict polarity and not the other.
all.poc: A null centered at 0; one observed draw lands in the opposite tail by chance. confirm_whole_corpus returns CONTRADICTED ('significant in the opposite tail') without measuring whether the contradiction reproduces across resamples, whereas the same magnitude in the supportive tail would be gated by stability_threshold; in adjudication it increments scorecard.n_contradicted.
all.suggested_fix: Apply stability symmetrically: when p_contra<=alpha compute contra-tail stability and require it to clear the threshold before returning 'contradicted'; if resamples are absent return 'indeterminate (stability not assessed)' for the contradiction too. Otherwise document in METHODOLOGY.md why contradictions are held to a weaker bar and surface the missing-stability caveat in the verdict evidence.

a.why:
Confirmed in current code at /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py. The asymmetry is exactly as described and is reproducible.

Code facts (confirm.py):
- Line 334-337: `if p_contra <= alpha: return Verdict.contradicted(...)` fires BEFORE any stability check. The contradiction branch precedes both the `stability is None` gate (338) and the `not stable` gate (345).
- Line 315: stability is computed only for `direction` (the predicted/support tail) via `_resample_stability(resample_statistics, null_distribution, direction, alpha)`. It is never computed for `opposite` (line 304). So contra-tail reproducibility is never measured at all.
- Line 345-350: the symmetric `supported` verdict requires `stable` (stability >= threshold), and line 338-344 makes `supported` unavailable when no resamples are supplied. The support polarity is gated; the contra polarity is not.

PoC (run with .venv/bin/python, PYTHONPATH=src) reproduces it precisely. Same magnitude, same unstable noise resamples (stability=0.04, far below the 0.9 threshold):
- observed=-3.0 (opposite tail) -> CONTRADICTED, notes 'significant in the opposite tail' (no stability gate; the 0.04 stability is computed for the wrong tail and ignored).
- observed=+3.0 (predicted tail) -> INDETERMINATE, notes 'unstable across resamples'.
- With no resamples at all: contradiction still fires CONTRADICTED; support returns INDETERMINATE 'stability not assessed'.

Downstream impact confirmed: adjudication.py:83-100 `_score` increments `n_contradicted` from VerdictLabel.CONTRADICTED and computes `alignment_rate = n_supported / (n_supported + n_contradicted)`. A non-reproducible opposite-tail blip therefore increments n_contradicted and biases alignment_rate (a published SourceScorecard metric, METHODOLOGY.md §5) downward, with no reproducibility check applied to that contradiction. pipeline.py:673-685 logs these as run metrics.

Not already-handled: the only mitigation is the docstring note (lines 283, 333) stating contradictions are reportable without stability. That is a function-level acknowledgement, not a methodology-level justification. METHODOLOGY.md §4 (lines 79-89) frames Supported and Contradicted symmetrically ('at the pre-specified threshold' for both) and explicitly cautions that disagreement is not automatically proof a claim is wrong — which is the rationale for the indeterminate verdict and which the contradiction path bypasses. The finding's core point (reproduction demanded for one polarity, not the other, biasing a real output metric) is therefore a genuine methodological inconsistency, not speculative and not fully intended/justified.

Severity medium rather than high: the behavior is reproducible and biases a core published metric, but (a) it requires the specific noisy-opposite-tail scenario to manifest, (b) it is partially documented as a deliberate choice in the function docstring, and (c) it is a correctness/methodology bias, not a crash, data-loss, or security issue. The finding's suggested fix (symmetric contra-tail stability gate, or an explicit METHODOLOGY.md justification + missing-stability caveat in the verdict evidence) is appropriate.

b.why:
Verified in current code at /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:333-337. confirm_whole_corpus returns Verdict.contradicted as soon as p_contra <= alpha (line 334), BEFORE any stability/resample check, whereas the supported verdict (lines 345-350) is gated on `stable` (stability >= threshold). Stability is computed only in the predicted/support tail: `_resample_stability(resample_statistics, null_distribution, direction, alpha)` at line 315 uses `direction`, never the opposite tail. The asymmetry is therefore exactly as described.

PoC reproduced with the project venv: a null centered at 0 (range -5.0..5.0), observed=-4.9 (deep in the opposite 'less' tail vs predicted 'greater'), with 200 noise resamples around 0. Result: label=CONTRADICTED, notes='significant in the opposite tail', p_contradict=0.029 (<=0.05), stability=0.0 — i.e. a hard contradiction returned with measured support-tail stability of 0.0 and zero check on whether the contradiction itself reproduces. The symmetric support cases both gate: support-tail observed=4.9 with the same non-reproducing resamples -> INDETERMINATE ('unstable across resamples'); with no resamples -> INDETERMINATE ('stability not assessed'). Confirms the verdict polarities are held to different bars.

Downstream impact is real: adjudication.py:91-92 computes alignment_rate = n_supported / (n_supported + n_contradicted), so each non-reproducible opposite-tail blip increments n_contradicted in SourceScorecard and biases alignment_rate downward, which is logged as a pipeline metric (pipeline.py:673-685). The behavior is documented in the docstring (confirm.py:282-287) and METHODOLOGY.md §5 (docs/METHODOLOGY.md:100) but neither states a rationale for holding contradictions to a weaker bar, so it is intentional-but-unjustified rather than a justified design choice.

Genuine concern for a tool whose value proposition is reproducibility-gated, blind, pre-registered inference; not speculative and not already-handled. Severity is medium rather than high because it does not crash, corrupt data, or expose security risk — it biases a reported frequency metric under noise, and the bias is bounded (a contradiction still requires genuine significance in the opposite tail at rate ~alpha, not arbitrary noise). The suggested fix (symmetric contra-tail stability gating, or 'indeterminate (stability not assessed)' when resamples absent, plus documenting/surfacing the caveat) is sound.

all.title: Direct confirm primitives default to a presence-only pre-registration gate (authority=None) — bypassable real-verification
all.description: _gate_preregistration falls back to require_locked (a pure presence check on locked_at/timestamp_proof) when authority is None, and authority defaults to None on confirm_unit_level and confirm_whole_corpus. These primitives are study-facing (the adjudication docstring instructs studies to call them inside confirm callables). A study confirmer that calls them without threading the authority gets only the sentinel check, which a hand-written timestamp_proof='x' satisfies — no content-binding, no timestamp attestation, no lock-before-confirm ordering. In the composed run_study pipeline the runner pre-verifies via require_preregistered (defense-in-depth there), but any study/test driving these primitives directly, or a future runner that forgets to pass authority, silently downgrades pre-registration to a no-op.
all.poc: Construct a Hypothesis, replace(locked_at='2020-01-01T00:00:00+00:00', timestamp_proof='not-a-real-proof'), then call confirm_whole_corpus(...) with authority omitted -> only require_locked runs -> the fake proof is accepted and a verdict is produced on a hypothesis whose lock was never cryptographically verified.
all.suggested_fix: Make authority a required keyword argument on confirm_unit_level and confirm_whole_corpus (no None default) so every confirmatory call performs full require_preregistered. If an unverified exploratory path must exist, gate it behind an explicit loudly-named flag (verify_preregistration=False), forbidden for EXTERNAL_CLAIM/DISCOVERY origins.

a.why:
The finding is accurate and the PoC reproduces exactly against the current code (3 hardening passes applied).

Code confirmation:
- confirm.py:39-53 — _gate_preregistration runs the full require_preregistered ONLY when authority is not None; otherwise it falls back to require_locked, which (confirm.py:56-70) is a pure presence check on hypothesis.locked (i.e. locked_at and timestamp_proof both non-None — see hypothesis.py:67-69). No content binding, no timestamp verification, no lock-before-confirm ordering.
- confirm.py:146 (confirm_unit_level) and confirm.py:270 (confirm_whole_corpus) both default authority to None, so the insecure presence-only gate is the DEFAULT behavior.
- These primitives are explicitly study-facing: adjudication.py:43-49 documents that the study owns the ClaimConfirmer and "the engine supplies the confirmatory primitives (confirm_whole_corpus / confirm_unit_level) the study uses inside it."

Reproduced PoC (PYTHONPATH=src, .venv/bin/python3.14): built a WHOLE_CORPUS, EXTERNAL_CLAIM Hypothesis, applied dataclasses.replace(locked_at='2020-01-01T00:00:00+00:00', timestamp_proof='not-a-real-proof'), then called confirm_whole_corpus(observed=10.0, null=[0.0]*100, alpha=0.05, resample_statistics=[10.0]*50) with authority OMITTED. Result: Verdict label=SUPPORTED on a hypothesis whose lock was a hand-written fake string. Contrast: passing authority=LocalHmacAuthority(...) raises PreRegistrationError ('proof is not a local-hmac v1 token (wrong prefix)'). So the omission silently downgrades the methodology-grade pre-registration gate to a no-op, exactly as described.

Severity = high (not critical): the as-shipped composed pipeline has defense-in-depth — the runners pre-verify with require_preregistered before confirming (pipeline.py:532, adjudication.py:248, discovery.py:147), so a default-wired run_study is not bypassable today. The exposure is a genuine API-design footgun on a security/integrity-critical gate: the secure path is opt-in and the insecure presence-only check is the default, so any study confirmer that drives these primitives directly without threading authority (the documented usage pattern), or any future runner that forgets the pre-check, silently accepts forged locks and mints SUPPORTED/CONTRADICTED verdicts. The suggested fix (make authority a required keyword arg; gate any unverified path behind a loudly-named, origin-restricted flag) is appropriate. Not speculative, not already-handled at the primitive level, and contrary to the project's own stated methodology (preregistration.py docstring: a sentinel lock makes the three verdicts decorative).

b.why:
Confirmed in current code and PoC reproduces.

Code facts (src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py): _gate_preregistration (lines 39-53) runs full require_preregistered only when authority is not None; otherwise it falls back to require_locked (lines 56-70), a pure presence check. confirm_unit_level (line 146) and confirm_whole_corpus (line 270) both default authority=None. Hypothesis.locked (hypothesis.py:76-78) is only `locked_at is not None and timestamp_proof is not None` — no crypto. So a hand-written timestamp_proof satisfies the gate when authority is omitted.

PoC reproduced live (.venv/bin/python, PYTHONPATH=src): a WHOLE_CORPUS Hypothesis with replace(locked_at='2020-01-01T00:00:00+00:00', timestamp_proof='not-a-real-proof'), then confirm_whole_corpus(..., authority omitted) -> VerdictLabel.SUPPORTED. No content-binding, no timestamp attestation, no lock-before-confirm ordering was checked. The test suite confirms the contrast: tests/test_preregistration.py:243-251 shows that WITH authority the same fake proof raises PreRegistrationError.

Genuine-concern assessment: This is a real insecure-by-default weakness. The primitives are explicitly study-facing (adjudication.py:43-53 docstring: studies call confirm_whole_corpus/confirm_unit_level inside their confirmer), and the engine's own canonical end-to-end example (tests/integration/test_methodology_e2e.py:67-70) calls confirm_whole_corpus WITHOUT authority and asserts SUPPORTED — so the exemplified/default usage exercises the no-op gate. Moreover the ClaimConfirmer protocol (adjudication.py:51-53) and the discovery confirmer (discovery.py:150) are never handed an authority, so a study confirmer structurally cannot thread one into the primitive; the confirm-path's real verification is absent by construction and relies entirely on the runner's separate require_preregistered pre-check.

Severity = medium (not high/critical) because the shipped composed runners DO provide defense-in-depth: adjudicate_with_baseline calls require_preregistered (adjudication.py:248) and discovery calls it (discovery.py:147) before invoking the study confirmer, so a real run_study/adjudicate pipeline is not exploitable without also defeating the runner. The exposure is to (a) studies/tests driving the primitives directly (which the integration test itself does), and (b) any future runner that forgets the pre-check. The finding is not speculative, not already-fully-handled at the primitive boundary, and the documented opt-out is a deliberate-but-unsafe default rather than a robust design. Suggested fix (make authority a required kwarg, or gate an exploratory path behind a loud verify_preregistration=False flag forbidden for EXTERNAL_CLAIM/DISCOVERY) is appropriate.

all.title: Operator username PII survives transcript scrubbing outside /Users path context (committed provenance leak)
all.description: The scrubber's operator-username rule only matches the username directly after a home-path prefix: re.compile(r"(/(?:Users|home)/)[^/\s\"']+") (confirmed at scripts/capture_transcripts.py:51). Committed transcripts embed lsof/ps output where the username appears as a bare process-owner column (e.g. 'Claude 647 thomasjones 86u IPv4 ...'); that bare form is not preceded by /Users/ or /home/, so it is never redacted. The username appears ~570 times in non-path contexts in the tracked transcripts/sessions/*.jsonl. This defeats #F-024 ('committing transcripts does not publish the operator's identity') and the ADR-0003 data-sovereignty posture for a repo intended for public release; the same gap leaves any other PII surfaced by command output unredacted.
all.poc: grep -oE '[A-Za-z]+ +[0-9]+ thomasjones' transcripts/sessions/*.jsonl prints lsof/ps lines the scrubber left intact; git ls-files --error-unmatch confirms the .jsonl is tracked/committed.
all.suggested_fix: Redact the operator username as a standalone token: derive the username at runtime (Path.home()/source dir) and add a word-boundary pattern re.compile(rf"\b{re.escape(user)}\b") in addition to the path-prefix rule, so it is masked in process listings, git config, and env dumps. Re-run the scrubber over already-committed transcripts and amend, since the leak is already in history.

a.why: Verified against current code and reproduced the PoC. scripts/capture_transcripts.py:51 is exactly re.compile(r"(/(?:Users|home)/)[^/\s\"']+"), which only redacts the username when it follows a /Users/ or /home/ path prefix. The committed transcript transcripts/sessions/8d54714a-d616-4911-a386-9b4b0f2ce74d.jsonl is git-tracked (git ls-files --error-unmatch succeeds; present in pass-1/2/3 commits). grep -oE '[A-Za-z]+ +[0-9]+ +thomasjones' prints lsof/ps process-owner-column lines (e.g. 'nClaude 647 thomasjones', 'ex 1573 thomasjones') with the username intact. All 572 occurrences of the bare username survive, matching the finding's ~570 estimate. This is not a scrubber-never-ran artifact: /Users/[REDACTED] appears 14,367 times and the literal /Users/thomasjones appears 0 times, proving the path rule and email rule both work; the gap is specifically the bare (non-path) token form the path-anchored regex cannot match. The module docstring (lines 11-13, 34-37) and cited #F-024 explicitly state the goal that committing transcripts must not publish the operator identity, so this is a genuine gap that defeats a stated guarantee, not intended design. The suggested fix (add a word-boundary \b<user>\b pattern derived at runtime, then re-scrub and amend history) is correct.

b.why: Confirmed in current code after 3 hardening passes. scripts/capture_transcripts.py:51 is exactly `re.compile(r"(/(?:Users|home)/)[^/\s\"']+")`, which only redacts a username immediately preceded by /Users/ or /home/. The PoC reproduces: `grep -oE '[A-Za-z]+ +[0-9]+ thomasjones' transcripts/sessions/*.jsonl` prints lsof/ps process-owner lines (e.g. 'Claude 647 thomasjones') the scrubber left intact. 572 bare-username occurrences exist in the working-tree transcript, and the committed HEAD blob also contains them (git show HEAD:... | grep ... > 0). The scrubber demonstrably ran on these files (14,347 '/Users/[REDACTED]' replacements and 7,297 total REDACTED tokens), proving the bare form is a true miss, not unprocessed input. git ls-files --error-unmatch confirms the .jsonl is tracked/committed, so the leak is in history. This defeats the scrubber's own stated objective in its docstring/comments (#F-024: 'committing transcripts ... does not also publish the operator's identity') and the ADR-0003 data-sovereignty posture for a repo intended for public release; the transcripts/README.md only claims credential redaction and a generic path/email caveat, so the bare-username exposure is an undisclosed gap, not intended design. The same regex gap leaves any other PII surfaced in raw command output (process listings, env dumps, git config) unredacted. Severity medium rather than high/critical: it is operator self-identifying PII (a username, partially inferable anyway from the retained project-dir name structure), not a security credential or third-party data; the suggested fix (runtime-derived word-boundary username pattern plus re-scrubbing committed history) is correct and proportionate.

all.title: _CONN_INIT_LOCKS and _POOLS_BY_CONN grow unboundedly — never pruned, keyed by raw conn_str
all.description: _CONN_INIT_LOCKS is a process-lifetime dict keyed by resolved conn_str with add-only lifecycle (never removed); _POOLS_BY_CONN is the same. conn_str distinctness includes any URL variation (host alias, optional params, credential rotation that changes userinfo, sslmode toggles). A long-lived process targeting many distinct DSNs (multi-tenant runner, test harness rotating DSNs, credential rotation) accumulates a Lock per distinct string and, for _POOLS_BY_CONN, a live ConnectionPool + worker thread per distinct string with no eviction — an unbounded cache and thread/connection leak. Credential rotation makes the SAME logical DB appear as a NEW conn_str, orphaning the old pool (atexit-closed but never reused/closed until exit).
all.poc: Loop setting ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL to N distinct valid loopback DSNs (e.g. differing in ?application_name=run_i) and call get_checkpointer() each iteration; len(_CONN_INIT_LOCKS) and len(_POOLS_BY_CONN) grow to N and worker-thread count climbs by one per distinct DSN, none reclaimed.
all.suggested_fix: Canonicalize the cache key (drop volatile query params / strip userinfo for the key while keeping it for connect) so cosmetic/credential changes map to one entry; cap _POOLS_BY_CONN with an LRU that pool.close()es and removes the evicted pool from _OPEN_POOLS; prune _CONN_INIT_LOCKS for keys no longer in _POOLS_BY_CONN.

a.why:
The mechanical facts are all accurate against current code. In src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py: _CONN_INIT_LOCKS (line 85) is created add-only in _conn_init_lock() (lines 188-194, never removed); _POOLS_BY_CONN (line 89) is written at line 298 and never evicted; the cache key is the raw conn_str from get_postgres_connection_string() (line 238), which only validates loopback via require_local_uri (src/assay_engine/_local.py — returns the URL unchanged, no canonicalization). Each distinct conn_str opens a live ConnectionPool with open=True + a worker thread (lines 266-278), registered in _OPEN_POOLS, closed only at atexit (line 184). The test at tests/test_persistence.py:460-473 confirms distinct conn_strs each get their own pool. So the PoC (loop N distinct DSNs) is reproducible and the structures grow to N with one pool/thread each, none reclaimed.

However this is NOT a genuine production-readiness concern in the current code; it is a speculative hardening nicety. (1) Scope: ADR-0003 (docs/decisions/ADR-0003-local-first-data-sovereign.md) defines a single-box, data-sovereign, single-operator deployment. ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL is a static env var, in practice set once per process; there is no multi-tenant runner (explicitly out of scope) and no code path that injects per-run query params like ?application_name=run_i. (2) The one-pool-per-distinct-conn_str design is the INTENDED, already-reviewed bound: the prior real issue (per-call pool leak, PASS-2 lines 294-302, confirmed LOW) was fixed by #144 precisely by keying a cache on conn_str, with the accepted invariant being 'bounds threads/connections at one pool per distinct conn_str.' The number of distinct conn_strs is the implicit bound and is effectively 1 in the documented deployment. (3) No in-scope caller varies the DSN within a live process — grep shows the only production callers go through configured_checkpointer with a static env. The triggering scenarios (multi-tenant, DSN-rotating harness, live credential rotation re-reading a changed os.environ) all contradict the documented single-DSN model; credential rotation in this architecture restarts the process. Unlike the PASS-2 finding which was triggerable by an in-scope per-study-run call pattern, G-008's leak requires usage the architecture explicitly excludes. The suggested LRU/canonicalization is reasonable defense-in-depth but not a production-readiness defect in the current code.

b.why:
The structural code facts in the finding are accurate: in src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py, _CONN_INIT_LOCKS (line 85, populated at 191-193) and _POOLS_BY_CONN (line 89, populated at 298) are process-lifetime, add-only dicts keyed by the raw resolved conn_str with NO canonicalization, NO LRU cap, and NO eviction. conn_str comes verbatim from get_postgres_connection_string() (line 154, ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL env) so cosmetic variants (?application_name=..., sslmode toggles, rotated userinfo) are distinct keys. Each distinct key builds a new psycopg_pool.ConnectionPool (its own background worker thread, lines 266-278), registered in _OPEN_POOLS and closed only at atexit (179-185, 168-176). The PoC is mechanically reproducible exactly as written.

However, it is NOT a genuine production-readiness problem in the CURRENT code for THIS product, and the triggering scenarios are speculative against the actual deployment model:

1. Every conn_str is forced through require_local_uri (line 155 -> src/assay_engine/_local.py:90), which rejects any non-loopback host. There is no multi-tenant/remote fan-out capability.
2. There is no multi-tenant model anywhere (grep for "tenant" in src returns nothing) and NO runtime code path that mutates ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL — only tests monkeypatch it. No caller in src outside the persistence package even invokes get_checkpointer/configured_checkpointer.
3. The documented and enforced deployment model (ADR-0003, module docstring lines 4-6, _DEFAULT_PG line 48 "postgresql://localhost:5432/assay") is a single on-box logical store. In normal operation conn_str is constant, so both dicts hold exactly ONE entry — that single-entry cache is the entire purpose of the #144 fast path (lines 240-245).

The "multi-tenant runner", "test harness rotating DSNs", and "credential rotation" triggers cited by the finding are not real code paths in this repo; they are hypothetical. Even under rare loopback credential rotation, growth is a handful of entries over a long process life with orphaned pools atexit-closed — not an operational leak. The "unbounded" growth is unbounded only in theory; in practice it is bounded to ~1. This is at most a low-value defense-in-depth/hardening nicety (canonicalizing the key, adding an LRU), not a production defect. Per the instruction to confirm only genuine, non-speculative concerns in current code, this does not meet the bar.

all.title: run_study never persists or correlates the provenance trail to its MLflow run (success and failure paths)
all.description: run_study records a hash-chained provenance trail and stores experiment_run_id inside the run_end entry (pipeline.py:721), but never writes the trail anywhere durable nor attaches it to the experiment run via tracker.log_artifact, even when a tracker and run_id are known. Persistence is opt-in (StudyResult.persist_trail() at :226 + a manual tracker.log_artifact). So the default governed run produces an MLflow run with metrics but no link back to the append-only trail, and the trail evaporates on process exit. The failure path is worse: the run_failed entry (pipeline.py:732) omits experiment_run_id even though run_id is in scope and the finally block terminates the MLflow run as FAILED (:754) — the case where correlation matters most leaves a FAILED run and an in-memory-only trail sharing no recorded id. GOVERNANCE §3 calls the trail 'the spine of reproducibility and of the independence claim'.
all.poc: run_study(plan, gate_handler=auto_approve, tracker=MlflowExperimentTracker()): on success the MLflow run has params/metrics but zero artifacts and no provenance.json; StudyResult.provenance is gone on exit. With a discover() that raises, the run is set FAILED but run_failed has no experiment_run_id — given the FAILED run id you cannot find its trail, and vice versa.
all.suggested_fix: When a tracker and run_id are present, have run_study persist the trail to a working path and tracker.log_artifact(run_id, path) on the success path (best-effort partial trail on failure), guarded like the other track() calls. Add experiment_run_id=run_id to the run_failed trail.record() payload (deterministic, preserves hash-determinism) so failed runs are equally correlatable across the trail and the experiment store.

a.why:
Verified against current code at /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/pipeline.py.

CONFIRMED facts in code:
1. run_study never calls persist_trail() or tracker.log_artifact() in its body. grep across pipeline.py shows persist_trail/log_artifact appear ONLY in StudyResult.persist_trail (line 226) and its docstring (line 232); the run_study body has none. The trail lives only as the in-memory tuple StudyResult.provenance (line 765). So a default governed run (tracker=MlflowExperimentTracker(), auto-approve gate) yields an MLflow run with params/metrics but zero artifacts and no provenance.json. The MlflowExperimentTracker DOES expose log_artifact (observability/tracking.py:92-93), so attachment is technically available but never invoked.
2. The success terminus run_end (lines 717-723) records experiment_run_id=run_id, deterministically linking the trail to the MLflow run.
3. The failure terminus run_failed (lines 731-737) records ONLY error_type/message/last_phase and OMITS experiment_run_id, even though run_id is in scope (declared :311, set :353) and the finally block marks the MLflow run FAILED (line 754). On failure the except block re-raises (line 748), so NO StudyResult is returned — the only surviving artifact is the caller-owned partial trail, whose run_failed entry has no recorded link to the FAILED MLflow run id. This is a genuine, reproducible asymmetry exactly as the PoC describes.

NUANCE / why LOW not higher:
- The "trail evaporates / no durable persistence by default" framing (Claim A) is INTENDED, documented design, not a defect. GOVERNANCE.md §3 (lines 102-105) explicitly states "An in-memory trail is still as trustworthy as its process" and that a caller may pass its own trail so a raising run leaves an auditable partial trail; the persist_trail docstring (lines 229-233) explicitly delegates durability and log_artifact attachment to the caller. So default in-memory behavior and caller-owned persistence is by-design, and that portion of the finding is an enhancement suggestion rather than a production-readiness bug.
- The concrete, real defect is the failure-path correlation asymmetry (Claim B): run_end embeds experiment_run_id but run_failed does not, despite run_id being available and the run being marked FAILED. This is a true, deterministic, trivially-fixable inconsistency (add experiment_run_id=run_id to the run_failed payload, preserving hash-determinism just as run_end does) and the suggested fix is correct.

Net: the finding is confirmed (the described code behavior is real and the PoC reproduces), but its severity is low — the durability portion is intended design, and the only genuine bug (failure-path run_id omission) is a minor correlation gap with no security/data-loss/availability impact, affecting auditability of failed runs only.

b.why: Verified against current code at /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/pipeline.py. (1) run_study never calls tracker.log_artifact: grep shows zero log_artifact references in pipeline.py despite the method existing on the tracker (tracking.py:92). The only trail persistence is StudyResult.persist_trail() (pipeline.py:226), which is opt-in/caller-driven; its docstring (line 232) explicitly instructs the CALLER to call tracker.log_artifact afterward. So a default run_study(..., tracker=MlflowExperimentTracker()) yields an MLflow run with params/metrics but no provenance artifact, and StudyResult.provenance is in-memory only — gone on process exit. PoC reproducible. (2) Success path: run_end (pipeline.py:717-723) does record experiment_run_id=run_id, but the experiment store has no reciprocal link (no artifact), so correlation is one-directional. (3) Failure path: run_failed (pipeline.py:731-737) payload is only error_type/message/last_phase — experiment_run_id is omitted even though run_id is in scope; meanwhile the finally block (lines 751-754) terminates the MLflow run as FAILED. So a FAILED run and its trail share no recorded id in either direction — the discover()-raises PoC is reproducible. The fix (best-effort log_artifact on success guarded like other track() calls + adding experiment_run_id=run_id to the run_failed record, which is deterministic and preserves hash-determinism) is correct and necessary. This is a genuine governance/reproducibility defect (GOVERNANCE §3 names the trail the spine of reproducibility), not speculative, not already-handled, not intended design.

all.title: numpy resample-stability path allocates a full R×N boolean matrix (peak-memory blowup) and lacks an at-scale equivalence/memory test
all.description: The numpy branch of _resample_stability computes null_arr[None,:] >= res_arr[:,None] (and the <= variant) before .sum(axis=1), materializing a dense R×N boolean array — O(R·N) PEAK MEMORY, whereas the pure-Python fallback it claims to merely speed up is O(N). Confirmed at confirm.py:216-217. At corpus scale (R=10k resamples vs N=10k permutation null = 10^8 booleans ≈ 100 MB transient; 20k×20k ≈ 400 MB) enabling the optional numpy extra silently raises peak memory by orders of magnitude and can OOM where the pure path would not, while the docstring frames it as 'purely a speed optimization, never a semantic change'. Compounding this, the only equivalence test (test_resample_stability_numpy_path_matches_pure_python, null=40/res=25) is far below any size where the mask is observable and pins no memory contract, so a future searchsorted/chunked rewrite has no at-scale guardrail.
all.poc: null=list(rng.normal(size=20000)); res=list(rng.normal(0.5,size=20000)); confirm._resample_stability(res, null, 'greater', 0.05) transiently allocates ~400MB (a 20000x20000 bool mask), whereas the pure path's working set is O(N). No existing test exercises this size or any memory characteristic.
all.suggested_fix: Sort the null once and use np.searchsorted for per-resample extreme counts in O((R+N)logN) time / O(R+N) memory: greater -> cnt = len(null) - searchsorted(null_sorted, res_arr, 'left'); less -> cnt = searchsorted(null_sorted, res_arr, 'right'); the (cnt+1)/(n+1)<=alpha test is byte-identical. If keeping the broadcast form, chunk the resamples and document the memory characteristic. Add a parametrized equivalence test at N=2000/R=2000 (and optionally a tracemalloc sub-quadratic peak-allocation regression test) so the rewrite is held to byte-identical results.

a.why:
Verified against current code. confirm.py:216-219 computes `(null_arr[None, :] >= res_arr[:, None]).sum(axis=1)` (and the `<=` variant for the 'less' tail), which materializes a dense R×N boolean array BEFORE the axis-1 reduction. numpy bool dtype is 1 byte/element, so R=N=20000 transiently allocates 4×10^8 bytes ≈ 400MB (10k×10k ≈ 100MB) — exactly as the finding states. The pure-Python fallback (confirm.py:223) streams one null pass per resample with an O(N) working set, so the numpy path is asymptotically worse in PEAK MEMORY, not merely faster. The docstring (lines 204-208) explicitly claims the numpy path is "purely a speed optimization for large R·N, never a semantic change," which is the precise asymmetry the finding flags: large R·N is where the blowup occurs.

Inputs are unbounded: resample_statistics and null_distribution are caller-supplied sequences with no size cap or chunking anywhere (confirm.py:310-315). The `baseline` optional extra installs numpy>=1.26 (pyproject.toml:68-69) and the prod extras bundle it (line 73), so this path is live in real deployments. The numpy import is try/except-guarded, so it only activates when the extra is present.

The only equivalence test (tests/test_confirm.py:191-208) runs at null=40/res=25 — far below any scale where the dense mask is observable — and pins no memory/peak-allocation contract, so a future searchsorted/chunked rewrite has no at-scale or memory guardrail. The suggested searchsorted fix is byte-identical: for 'greater', count(null >= res) = len(null) - searchsorted(null_sorted, res, 'left'); for 'less', count(null <= res) = searchsorted(null_sorted, res, 'right'); both feed the same (cnt+1)/(n+1) <= alpha test.

PoC could not be executed directly because numpy is not installed in this verification sandbox (python3 -c 'import numpy' -> ModuleNotFoundError), but the broadcast semantics and 1-byte bool dtype make the allocation deterministic and the memory arithmetic correct; the code path is unambiguous.

Severity is medium, not high: it requires BOTH the optional baseline extra AND corpus-scale R·N to manifest, the impact is a transient memory degradation / OOM risk rather than any correctness, semantic, or security defect (results remain identical), and the workaround (omit the extra) restores the O(N) path. It is a genuine production-readiness/scalability concern with a missing test contract, not speculative or intended design.

b.why: Verified against current code at /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:216-217. The numpy branch computes `(null_arr[None,:] >= res_arr[:,None]).sum(axis=1)` (and the `<=` variant), which materializes a dense R×N boolean array before reducing — O(R·N) peak memory, versus the O(N) working set of the pure-Python fallback at confirm.py:222-224. numpy 2.4.6 is installed in the project's .venv (Python 3.14.5), so this is the live execution path, not a theoretical/dead branch. PoC reproduced exactly with the venv interpreter: null=res=20000 yields a measured tracemalloc peak of 406.2 MB, matching the predicted 20000*20000 = 400 MB boolean mask; the searchsorted alternative peaks at 0.87 MB (~460x lower). The docstring at confirm.py:204-208 explicitly claims the numpy path is "purely a speed optimization for large R·N, never a semantic change," which is misleading precisely in the large-R·N regime where it silently raises peak memory by orders of magnitude and can OOM where the pure path would not. The only equivalence test (tests/test_confirm.py:191-208) uses null=40/res=25 — far below any size where the mask is observable — and pins no memory contract, so a future rewrite has no at-scale guardrail. I independently confirmed the suggested np.searchsorted fix is byte-identical to the current numpy path across 1200 parametrized cases (0 mismatches). This is a true, reproducible production concern, not speculative or already-handled. Severity is medium rather than high because it manifests only when the optional numpy extra is installed AND R·N is large, and it is a transient peak-memory/OOM-risk rather than a correctness defect (computed results are correct in all cases).

all.title: discover_and_confirm does not type-guard the confirmer's return value (asymmetric with adjudication/pipeline)
all.description: In adjudicate_with_baseline (adjudication.py:253) and the pipeline runner (pipeline.py:563) a confirmer that forgets to return (yields None) is caught with an explicit isinstance(verdict, Verdict) guard (#F-021). The standalone discovery runner was left out: after verdict = confirm(hypothesis, held_out) (discovery.py:150) it goes straight to verdict.hypothesis_id (:151, which only checks id-mismatch, not type). A confirmer returning None raises an opaque AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'hypothesis_id' instead of the documented typed FirewallViolation. The module docstring (lines 94-97) promises FirewallViolation on a verdict<->hypothesis misattribution, and a None verdict never reaches that check, breaking the typed-exception contract for one of the two public Firewall-B entry points.
all.poc: def bad_confirm(hyp, held_out): pass  # returns None. discover_and_confirm(corpus, split, discover=..., confirm=bad_confirm, authority=...) raises AttributeError("'NoneType' object has no attribute 'hypothesis_id'") instead of FirewallViolation('confirm returned NoneType ... expected a Verdict').
all.suggested_fix: Mirror the adjudication/pipeline guard immediately after confirm(): if not isinstance(verdict, Verdict): raise FirewallViolation(f"confirm returned {type(verdict).__name__} for hypothesis {hypothesis.hypothesis_id!r} — expected a Verdict"). Verdict is already imported (line 39).

a.why: Verified in current code. src/assay_engine/methodology/discovery.py:150 does `verdict = confirm(hypothesis, held_out)` and proceeds directly to `if verdict.hypothesis_id != hypothesis.hypothesis_id:` at :151 with no type guard. Both peer confirm sites have an explicit guard: adjudication.py:253 (`if not isinstance(verdict, Verdict):` raising FirewallViolation, citing pass-3 #F-021) and the pipeline runner at src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:563 (identical guard). The finding cited pipeline.py:563 as a different path, but the guard exists there as described; only discovery.py lacks it, so the asymmetry across the public Firewall-B entry points is real. The module docstring (discovery.py:94-97) promises FirewallViolation on a verdict↔hypothesis misattribution, and a None verdict never reaches that typed check. Verdict is already imported at discovery.py:39, so the suggested one-line fix applies cleanly. PoC reproduced: a confirmer returning None (def bad_confirm(hyp, held_out): pass) makes discover_and_confirm raise `AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'hypothesis_id'` instead of the documented typed FirewallViolation. Severity is low: it only triggers on an author-supplied buggy confirmer; the process still halts loudly (no silent corruption, no firewall bypass). The concrete harm is an opaque error message and a broken typed-exception contract inconsistency relative to the other two confirm sites — a real production-readiness/robustness defect, not speculative or already-handled.

b.why:
Verified against current code at /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay. The asymmetry is real and the PoC reproduces exactly.

Evidence:
- discovery.py:150 `verdict = confirm(hypothesis, held_out)` is followed immediately by discovery.py:151 `if verdict.hypothesis_id != hypothesis.hypothesis_id:` — an id-mismatch check only, with NO type guard.
- adjudication.py:253 has `if not isinstance(verdict, Verdict): raise FirewallViolation(...)` (the #F-021 pattern), and the pipeline runner has the same guard at src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:563. discovery.py is the only one of the three confirm-seam call sites lacking it (grep for `isinstance(verdict, Verdict)` returns only adjudication.py).
- The module docstring (discovery.py:94-97) promises FirewallViolation on a verdict<->hypothesis misattribution; a None verdict never reaches that typed check.
- `Verdict` is imported at discovery.py:39, so the suggested fix is directly applicable.
- discover_and_confirm is a public export (methodology/__init__.py:22,61), one of the two public Firewall-B runners.

PoC reproduced (with the upstream require_preregistered gate stubbed to a no-op, since it is not the seam under test): a `confirm` returning None raises `AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'hypothesis_id'` instead of the documented typed FirewallViolation. Confirmed via the project venv (.venv/bin/python; system python3 is 3.9 and cannot load slots= dataclasses).

Minor finding-text inaccuracy: pipeline.py lives at src/assay_engine/pipeline.py, not src/assay_engine/methodology/pipeline.py; line 563 is correct. Does not affect substance.

Severity: low. The defect is a real contract-consistency divergence from an invariant the codebase explicitly established and enforced in two sibling paths, but impact is limited: it only triggers when a study author writes a buggy confirm callable that forgets to return, and the failure is still loud (an exception is raised) — just untyped/opaque rather than the documented FirewallViolation. No silent firewall bypass, no data corruption.

all.title: from_records raises raw KeyError on a malformed record instead of the typed ProvenanceError
all.description: from_records rebuilds ProvenanceEntry by directly subscripting each record (r['seq'], r['kind'], r['summary'], r['payload'], r['timestamp'], r['prev_hash'], r['entry_hash'] at provenance.py:266-275, confirmed). A record deserialized from an external/persistent store missing any key raises a bare KeyError, not the module's typed ProvenanceError.
