Every other deserialization/verification path here surfaces ProvenanceError (verify_records, _digest, clock/timestamp guards, freeze guard). The trail is explicitly a trust-boundary deserialization point ('rebuild entries ... and verify the chain'), so an externally-supplied record dropping/renaming a field should fail with the trail's typed error catchable alongside verify_records failures; a non-int seq / non-str field would also propagate untyped.
all.poc: from_records([{ 'seq':0,'kind':'x','summary':'s','payload':{}, 'timestamp':'2020-01-01T00:00:00+00:00','prev_hash':'assay-provenance:v1:genesis' }]) -> KeyError: 'entry_hash' (expected ProvenanceError describing the malformed record).
all.suggested_fix: Wrap the construction in try/except (KeyError, TypeError, ValueError) as exc: raise ProvenanceError(f'provenance record could not be rebuilt: {exc}') from None, then call verify_records on the typed-good entries.

a.why: Verified against current code. src/assay_engine/provenance.py:266-277 rebuilds ProvenanceEntry by direct subscript (r["seq"], r["kind"], r["summary"], r["payload"], r["timestamp"], r["prev_hash"], r["entry_hash"]) inside from_records, with no try/except. The PoC reproduces exactly: from_records([{seq,kind,summary,payload,timestamp,prev_hash}]) (missing entry_hash) raises raw KeyError('entry_hash'), not ProvenanceError (confirmed by running it under python3.14 with PYTHONPATH=src). from_records is an explicit deserialization/trust-boundary entry point ("Rebuild entries from ... and verify the chain"), and every other failure mode on this path (verify_records seq/linkage/hash mismatch at lines 248-256, _digest, clock/freeze guards) surfaces the module's typed ProvenanceError (defined line 49). A caller catching ProvenanceError to handle a corrupt/malformed external trail will not catch a dropped/renamed field; a non-int seq or non-str field would likewise propagate an untyped TypeError/ValueError through verify_records/_digest. The suggested fix (wrap construction in try/except (KeyError, TypeError, ValueError) -> raise ProvenanceError(...) from None, then verify_records) is correct. This is a real, reproducible, non-speculative concern, but it is an exception-type/consistency robustness gap at a trust boundary rather than a security bypass or integrity failure — chain integrity is still enforced by verify_records once entries are constructed; only structurally malformed records leak an untyped exception.

b.why: Verified against current code. src/assay_engine/provenance.py:266-277 rebuilds ProvenanceEntry by directly subscripting r["seq"], r["kind"], r["summary"], r["payload"], r["timestamp"], r["prev_hash"], r["entry_hash"] with no try/except, then calls verify_records. PoC reproduced exactly with the venv interpreter: from_records([...]) on a record missing entry_hash raises a bare `KeyError: 'entry_hash'`, NOT ProvenanceError. The module otherwise consistently surfaces the typed ProvenanceError(RuntimeError) at every other validation/deserialization point (provenance.py:85,119,131,133,138,143,156,176 and verify_records at 249,251,254). from_records is a documented trust-boundary deserialization entry point ("Rebuild entries from ProvenanceTrail.to_records output and verify the chain") consuming external/persisted records, so a dropped/renamed key (KeyError) or a wrong-typed value (TypeError/ValueError) leaks an untyped exception that callers cannot catch alongside verify_records failures, breaking the module's error-contract convention. Not speculative, not already-handled, not intended design. Severity is low: it is an error-typing/robustness defect at a deserialization boundary, not an integrity/security defect — the HMAC chain cannot be forged because verify_records still rejects any entry that does successfully construct. Suggested fix (wrap construction in try/except (KeyError, TypeError, ValueError) -> raise ProvenanceError(...) from None) is appropriate.

all.title: test_cosine_matrix_scale_uses_fast_path passes even if the numpy fast path is reverted to the pure O(n^2*d) path
all.description: The test claims to guard against 'a regression to the pure O(n^2*d) path at corpus scale', but its only fast-path-discriminating assertion is assert elapsed < 2.0; the rest (len==300, m[0][0]==1.0) hold for either path. The pure-Python _cosine_matrix_pure on a 300x64 matrix completes in ~0.08s — ~24x under the 2.0s threshold. Reverting cosine_similarity_matrix (primitives.py:155) to always call _cosine_matrix_pure leaves this test GREEN. The test does not discriminate the fix it names, and the loose wall-clock budget would not catch a real regression on a larger corpus either (and is flaky on loaded CI).
all.poc: Change cosine_similarity_matrix (primitives.py:153-155) to unconditionally return _cosine_matrix_pure(rows); the test still passes (300x64 pure runtime ~0.08s < 2.0s, m[0][0]==1.0, dims 300x300).
all.suggested_fix: Assert the fast path is taken rather than timing it: monkeypatch _cosine_matrix_pure to record/raise and assert it is NOT called when numpy is installed, or spy on _cosine_matrix_numpy and assert it WAS called. Drop the brittle non-discriminating elapsed < 2.0 assertion.

a.why: Verified against current code at /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay (note: package is assay_engine, not hc_assay; finding's line/path references otherwise accurate). The test at tests/test_baseline.py:237-251 documents itself as guarding "against a regression to the pure O(n^2*d) path at corpus scale," but none of its assertions discriminate the path: (1) dims len==300/len(m[0])==300 are identical for both paths; (2) m[0][0]==1.0 holds for the pure path too because _cosine_matrix_pure explicitly sets the non-zero self-diagonal to exactly 1.0 (primitives.py:80-81); (3) elapsed<2.0 is non-discriminating because the pure path on the exact 300x64 matrix the test builds runs in ~0.084s (measured), ~24x under the 2.0s budget. PoC reproduced end-to-end: patching cosine_similarity_matrix (primitives.py:155) to unconditionally return _cosine_matrix_pure(rows) leaves the test GREEN (1 passed in 0.12s); restored cleanly afterward (git diff empty). The wall-clock budget is also brittle/flaky under CI load and would not reliably catch a real large-corpus regression. This is a genuine test-quality defect, not intended design or already-handled. Severity low: it is a defective regression guard (false coverage), but the shipped production code (cosine_similarity_matrix correctly dispatches to numpy) is itself correct, so end-user-facing behavior is unaffected.

b.why:
Confirmed by direct reproduction in current code.

Code matches the finding exactly:
- src/assay_engine/baseline/primitives.py:139-155 — cosine_similarity_matrix dispatches to _cosine_matrix_numpy(rows) at line 155 when numpy is importable, else _cosine_matrix_pure.
- tests/test_baseline.py:237-251 — test_cosine_matrix_scale_uses_fast_path. Its only fast-path-discriminating assertion is `assert elapsed < 2.0` (line 251). The other assertions (`len(m) == 300 and len(m[0]) == 300` at 249, `m[0][0] == pytest.approx(1.0)` at 250) hold for either path. (Note the module path in the finding text said primitives.py:155 but referenced the wrong package; the actual file is src/assay_engine/baseline/primitives.py — the test imports from assay_engine.baseline.primitives.)

Empirical PoC reproduction:
1. Measured _cosine_matrix_pure on the test's exact 300x64 matrix: ~0.086s — roughly 23x under the 2.0s budget; len==300x300; m[0][0]==1.0. All three structural/timing assertions pass under the pure path.
2. Patched line 155 to unconditionally `return _cosine_matrix_pure(rows)` and ran the actual test: `1 passed in 0.12s`. Restored the file (verified clean).

So the test does NOT discriminate the regression it names ("guards against a regression to the pure O(n^2*d) path at corpus scale"): reverting the numpy dispatch leaves it green. The wall-clock budget is both non-discriminating at this corpus size and brittle on loaded CI (a real performance guard should assert the numpy path is invoked, e.g. spy on _cosine_matrix_numpy, or scale the matrix until the pure path provably exceeds the budget).

Severity: low. This is a test-quality / false-confidence defect, not a production runtime bug — the shipped dispatch is correct; only the guard test fails to protect it. Genuine (not speculative, not intended design, not already handled), so confirmed.

all.title: test_install_flush_on_exit_wires_atexit_and_sigterm passes even if the SIGTERM handler wiring (#O3) is fully reverted
all.description: The test asserts BOTH atexit and the SIGTERM handler are wired, but the SIGTERM half is asserted only inside `if "h" in captured:` (tests/test_observability.py:188-190). captured['h'] is populated solely as a side effect of the source calling signal.signal(SIGTERM, _handler) (tracing.py:141). If a developer reverts the SIGTERM block (tracing.py:131-141) keeping only atexit.register (:130), signal.signal is never invoked, captured stays empty, the if-branch is skipped, and the test passes on the atexit assertions alone. The SIGTERM portion of this regression guard is vacuous on the exact revert it is meant to catch — the conditional turns a should-be-required assertion into a no-op when the fix is missing.
all.poc: Delete the `if threading.current_thread() is threading.main_thread(): ... signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, _handler)` block (tracing.py:131-141), keep atexit.register(lambda: _safe(flush)). The test still passes: atexit handler registered and flush>=1; captured is empty so the SIGTERM assertions are skipped.
all.suggested_fix: Since pytest runs the body on the main thread, unconditionally assert 'h' in captured and exercise it: captured['h'](signal.SIGTERM, None); assert calls['shutdown'] >= 1. Remove the if-guard so a reverted handler fails the test.

a.why: Verified against current code at /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay (source is src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py, not src/hc_assay/... as the finding stated, but the line numbers and content match exactly). tests/test_observability.py:188 wraps the SIGTERM assertions (lines 189-190) in `if "h" in captured:`. captured['h'] is set only as a side effect of the source calling signal.signal at tracing.py:141 inside the main-thread block (tracing.py:131-143). I reproduced the PoC: removed the entire `if threading.current_thread() is threading.main_thread(): ... signal.signal(...)` block while keeping atexit.register(lambda: _safe(flush)) at :130, then ran the test with the project venv pytest — it PASSED (1 passed). With the SIGTERM block reverted, signal.signal is never called, captured stays empty, the if-branch is skipped, and the test passes on the atexit assertions alone. Restored source; git diff clean; test still passes normally. Because pytest executes the test body on the main thread, signal.signal is ALWAYS invoked in correct code, so the conditional has no legitimate purpose and only masks the exact regression it should catch — the SIGTERM half of this guard is vacuous on revert. The suggested fix (unconditionally assert 'h' in captured, invoke captured['h'](SIGTERM, None), assert calls['shutdown'] >= 1) is correct. This is a genuine, non-speculative regression-guard defect. The production #O3 code itself is correct; the concern is that a future revert losing SIGTERM/container-stop span flush would not be caught by CI. That makes it a real but indirect production-readiness issue, hence medium severity rather than high.

b.why:
VERIFIED against current code at /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay (note: source path is src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py, not src/hc_assay/... as the finding's prose header implies, but the content matches exactly).

Code facts confirmed:
- tests/test_observability.py:188-190 asserts the SIGTERM half ONLY inside `if "h" in captured:`. captured['h'] is set solely by the monkeypatched signal.signal (line 180), which the source invokes at tracing.py:141.
- tracing.py:130 = atexit.register(lambda: _safe(flush)); tracing.py:131-143 = the main-thread-guarded SIGTERM block that calls signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, _handler). The handler calls force_flush + shutdown (#O3).

PoC reproduced empirically: I deleted lines 131-143 (the entire `if threading.current_thread() is threading.main_thread(): ... signal.signal(...)` block), keeping only atexit.register, then ran `.venv/bin/pytest tests/test_observability.py::test_install_flush_on_exit_wires_atexit_and_sigterm` -> 1 passed. With the original code restored (git diff --stat clean) the test also passes. So the test is green BOTH with and without the SIGTERM fix — the SIGTERM regression guard is vacuous on the exact revert it is meant to catch.

Root cause confirmed: signal.signal is never called when the block is reverted, captured stays empty, the if-branch is skipped, and only the atexit assertions (which still hold) run. The conditional turns a should-be-required assertion into a no-op.

Suggested fix is sound: pytest executes the body on the main thread (verified threading.current_thread() is threading.main_thread() == True), so when the fix is present captured['h'] is always populated. Removing the if-guard and unconditionally asserting 'h' in captured + exercising captured['h'](SIGTERM, None) + asserting calls['shutdown'] >= 1 would fail on the revert.

Severity: medium. This is a real test-effectiveness defect (a guard test that does not guard the seam it claims to protect — silently lost provenance spans on container SIGTERM would regress undetected), but it is a test-quality/regression-coverage gap, not a runtime defect in shipped behavior. The production SIGTERM flush code itself is currently correct.

all.title: SBOM step re-runs pip-audit without the allowlist, so an accepted advisory fails CI
all.description: The audit job's vuln gate (ci.yml:113-127) builds an IGNORES array from .pip-audit-ignore and passes --ignore-vuln to pip-audit. The next step 'SBOM (CycloneDX) from the lockfile' (ci.yml:135) runs pip-audit ... --format cyclonedx-json under set -euo pipefail WITHOUT any --ignore-vuln args (confirmed). pip-audit exits non-zero on any found vulnerability regardless of output format, so any advisory the team has consciously allowlisted passes the gate step but FAILS the SBOM step — bricking CI on exactly the condition the allowlist exists to handle, and never emitting the SBOM when it is most needed. The allowlist is effectively a no-op once any accepted advisory is added.
all.poc: Add a real advisory id (present for a requirements.lock-pinned package) to .pip-audit-ignore. The 'pip-audit (reproducible known-vulnerability scan)' step passes (allowlisted); the 'SBOM' step runs pip-audit without --ignore-vuln, exits 1 on the same advisory, set -e aborts, the audit job fails, and all-checks blocks the merge.
all.suggested_fix: Factor the IGNORES array into a shared function and reuse it in the SBOM invocation, or decouple SBOM generation from the vuln scan (cyclonedx-py requirements requirements.lock, or pass --ignore-vuln to the cyclonedx invocation). The SBOM should describe the dependency set independent of accepted advisories.

a.why: Confirmed in current code. In .github/workflows/ci.yml the vuln-scan step (lines 113-127) builds an IGNORES array from .pip-audit-ignore and passes it to pip-audit at line 127: `pip-audit --strict --require-hashes --progress-spinner off -r requirements.lock "${IGNORES[@]}"`. The immediately following SBOM step (lines 128-137) re-invokes pip-audit at line 135 WITHOUT any --ignore-vuln args: `pip-audit --progress-spinner off -r requirements.lock --format cyclonedx-json -o sbom.cyclonedx.json`, and the whole step runs under `set -euo pipefail` (line 130). pip-audit's exit code is determined by whether vulnerabilities are found, not by the --format/-o output mode, so any advisory the team allowlists via .pip-audit-ignore will pass the gate step (line 127) but still be detected at line 135, causing a non-zero exit, set -e abort, audit-job failure, and a blocked merge — and no SBOM artifact is produced precisely when an advisory is present. The design intent is explicit in the comment at lines 87-88: the allowlist exists to cover "accepted/unfixable" advisories; this step silently defeats that intent. The PoC is reproducible exactly as written.\n\nGenuineness/severity calibration: This is a real defect, not speculative or intended design, and the fix (share the IGNORES array or decouple SBOM generation from the vuln scan via cyclonedx-py / --ignore-vuln) is correct. However it is currently DORMANT: .pip-audit-ignore does not exist in the repo (confirmed: file absent), so no advisory is presently allowlisted and CI is not currently red. The bug manifests only the first time the allowlist is used as designed. It degrades the CI/supply-chain pipeline (breaks CI and suppresses the SBOM) rather than introducing a runtime/production-security vulnerability, so it is not high/critical.

b.why: Verified against current code at /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/.github/workflows/ci.yml. The vuln-gate step (lines 113-127) builds an IGNORES array from .pip-audit-ignore and passes "${IGNORES[@]}" (i.e. --ignore-vuln entries) to pip-audit at line 127. The very next step "SBOM (CycloneDX) from the lockfile" (lines 128-137) runs at line 135: `pip-audit --progress-spinner off -r requirements.lock --format cyclonedx-json -o sbom.cyclonedx.json` with NO --ignore-vuln args, under `set -euo pipefail` (line 130). The comment at lines 132-134 explicitly removed the `|| true` mask so a pip-audit failure must fail the step. pip-audit's exit code reflects whether vulnerabilities were found regardless of --format (the format only changes rendering, not exit semantics); --ignore-vuln is the only mechanism that suppresses an advisory from the non-zero exit. Therefore any advisory consciously allowlisted in .pip-audit-ignore passes the gate (line 127, allowlist applied) but is re-discovered by the SBOM step (line 135, allowlist NOT applied), which exits 1, set -e aborts the audit job, and all-checks blocks the merge. The allowlist mechanism is thus defeated the moment it is used for its only purpose, and the SBOM is never emitted precisely when an accepted advisory exists. The PoC is reproducible as described. Caveat: .pip-audit-ignore does not currently exist in the repo, so no advisory is allowlisted today and CI is currently green — this is a latent trap, not an actively-firing failure. That conditionality lowers the severity but does not invalidate the finding; it is a real design flaw, not speculative or intended. Suggested fix (factor IGNORES into shared logic, or decouple SBOM via cyclonedx-py, or pass --ignore-vuln to the SBOM invocation) is appropriate.

all.title: Dependabot bumps pyproject ranges but cannot regenerate the hash-pinned lockfiles the CI gate enforces, so its PRs fail the lockfile-sync gate; uv.lock is unmanaged
all.description: The pip ecosystem entry lets Dependabot bump pyproject.toml constraints, but the audit job (ci.yml:98-112) enforces that requirements.lock and requirements-core.lock are byte-for-byte regenerable from pyproject via uv pip compile. Dependabot has no hook to run uv pip compile, so any Dependabot PR changing a pyproject constraint leaves the committed lockfiles stale and FAILS the lockfile-sync gate -> all-checks blocks the merge -> the dependency-update PRs are unmergeable without manual intervention every time. Most runtime deps live in optional-extras with floors-only/range pins (langgraph>=1.2,<2), so Dependabot may not even open PRs while installed transitive versions drift. Additionally uv.lock is committed but referenced by nothing in CI (the gate compiles to requirements*.lock), an orphaned unverified lock artifact that can diverge unnoticed.
all.poc: Wait for (or simulate) a weekly Dependabot pip PR bumping a pyproject constraint without regenerating locks. The 'Lockfiles are in sync with pyproject' step diffs requirements.lock against a fresh uv pip compile and exits 1 ('::error::requirements.lock is stale'); all-checks fails; the PR cannot merge.
all.suggested_fix: Either add a CI/scheduled post-update step (or Dependabot-compatible action) that runs uv pip compile for both lockfiles and commits the result, or switch the supply-chain workflow to manage/audit uv.lock directly (uv-native) and drop the orphaned lock. Document which lock is canonical and enforce exactly one; consider versioning-strategy/grouping to batch lock regeneration.

a.why:
Verified against current code on branch feat/reasoning-client-agent-execution.

(1) The lockfile-sync gate is real and strict. ci.yml:98-112 ("Lockfiles are in sync with pyproject") installs uv==0.11.21, runs `uv pip compile pyproject.toml --all-extras --universal --generate-hashes -o /tmp/requirements.regen.lock`, then `diff` (header-stripped) against the committed requirements.lock and `exit 1` with `::error::requirements.lock is stale` on any difference; it repeats the same for requirements-core.lock (--extra dev). This audit job feeds all-checks (ci.yml:171, needs:[core,integration,audit]; ci.yml:176-183 fails if any is not success), which the project intends as the single required status check (ci.yml:165-168). So a stale lock blocks merge.

(2) Dependabot has no lock-regeneration hook. .github/dependabot.yml:8 declares `package-ecosystem: pip` against pyproject.toml with weekly schedule and minor/patch grouping, but the file contains no post-update step, and the only CI workflow is ci.yml (no automation/recompile workflow exists — grep over .github/workflows found nothing running `uv pip compile` outside the gate itself). Dependabot edits pyproject.toml constraints; it cannot run `uv pip compile`. Therefore any Dependabot PR that changes a pyproject constraint leaves requirements.lock / requirements-core.lock unregenerated → the sync gate diff fails → all-checks blocks → the PR is unmergeable without manual `uv pip compile` + commit. PoC is reproducible exactly as described.

(3) uv.lock is an orphaned artifact. It is committed (1,071,427 bytes, present at repo root) but referenced by nothing in CI or scripts — grep across *.yml/*.yaml/*.sh/*.py/*.toml/Makefile found references only in docs/hardening/PASS-1.md and PASS-2.md. The gate compiles to requirements*.lock, not uv.lock, so uv.lock is unverified and can diverge silently.

(4) Drift surface confirmed: runtime deps live in [project.optional-dependencies] with floor+ceiling range pins (pyproject.toml: langgraph>=1.2,<2 line 60, psycopg>=3.2,<4 line 62, mlflow>=3.1,<4 line 57, etc.); top-level `dependencies = []` (line 31). Within those ranges, transitive/in-range patch versions can drift without Dependabot raising the declared constraint, while the locks remain the source of installed truth — reinforcing that the canonical lock must be machine-maintained.

This is a genuine, non-speculative process/CI break (not intended design): the dependency-update automation the project added (#147) is structurally incompatible with the reproducibility gate it added (#F-031), and there is a redundant unverified lock. Severity is medium: it does not affect runtime/security of shipped code, but it makes the Dependabot pipeline functionally non-operational (every dep PR requires manual lock regeneration) and leaves an unverified committed artifact, defeating the stated supply-chain-monitoring purpose.

b.why: Independently verified against current code. (1) The lockfile-sync gate exists exactly as described: ci.yml:98-112 runs `uv pip compile` for both requirements.lock (--all-extras) and requirements-core.lock (--extra dev) and diffs against the committed files, exiting 1 with `::error::requirements.lock is stale`. This step is in the `audit` job, which feeds `all-checks` (ci.yml:171), the single required merge gate. (2) dependabot.yml:8 enables the pip ecosystem (weekly), but there is NO post-update hook to regenerate the hash-pinned lockfiles: ci.yml is the only workflow, and grep finds no `uv pip compile`, no scheduled/workflow_dispatch lock-regen, and no dependabot-author auto-commit step. Therefore any Dependabot PR that edits a pyproject constraint leaves requirements*.lock stale -> sync gate fails -> all-checks blocks merge -> the PR is unmergeable without manual `uv pip compile`. PoC reproducible. (3) uv.lock is committed (1MB) but referenced by nothing in CI/scripts (grep only hits docs/hardening/PASS-*.md); no `uv lock --check`/`uv sync --frozen` validation, so it is an orphaned, unverified artifact that can drift silently. (4) pyproject.toml uses range pins for optional-extras deps (e.g. langgraph>=1.2,<2, lines 48-69) with core dependencies=[], matching the drift concern. The finding is a genuine process/supply-chain-automation defect, not speculative and not already handled. It is not a runtime/security vulnerability, hence medium rather than high.

all.title: Unit-level confirm validates predicted_direction shape but never uses it, leaving direction_supports_claim unverified
all.description: confirm_unit_level re-validates that hypothesis.predicted_direction is in {'greater','less'} as defense-in-depth, but the unit-level verdict is decided entirely by the trusted caller flag direction_supports_claim and never cross-checked against the pre-registered predicted_direction. A hypothesis locked with predicted_direction='greater' can be confirmed 'supported' on direction_supports_claim=True even when the held-out statistic points the other way — the engine binds a direction into the proof then ignores it at decision time. The validation at lines 162-168 gives a false sense the locked direction governs the verdict; it does not. Smaller than G-001 (requires a dishonest caller flag rather than free post-hoc choice) but the locked direction provides zero decision-time guarantee for the unit-level path.
all.poc: Hypothesis locked predicted_direction='greater'. Caller passes direction_supports_claim=True with a statistic actually lower than baseline. verdict_from_pvalue returns SUPPORTED purely on the flag; the locked 'greater' is never consulted.
all.suggested_fix: Require the caller to pass the observed comparison sign and derive supports = (observed_relation == locked_direction) inside the engine rather than accepting a free boolean. At minimum, when predicted_direction is locked, validate direction_supports_claim is consistent with caller-supplied directional evidence and record both in the verdict.

a.why: TRUE and reproducible in current code. confirm_unit_level (src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:136-179) takes direction_supports_claim:bool as a trusted caller input (line 145) and the validation at lines 162-168 only checks predicted_direction is a recognized string in _VALID_DIRECTIONS — it never cross-checks the locked direction against direction_supports_claim. The verdict is decided entirely by p_value<=alpha and the flag in verdict_from_pvalue (lines 129-132); the docstring at lines 112-113 explicitly states statistic is "recorded for provenance only (it does NOT gate the verdict)". So a hypothesis locked predicted_direction='greater' confirmed with direction_supports_claim=True returns SUPPORTED even when the held-out statistic points lower — the PoC holds exactly. The asymmetry the finding cites is real: confirm_whole_corpus genuinely derives the verdict from data via _empirical_p(null, observed, direction) against the locked direction (lines 303-306), giving the locked direction decision-time force, while the unit-level path consults the locked direction only for string-validation, never for the verdict. confirm_unit_level is also a bare public primitive (no runner in discovery.py/adjudication.py derives the direction for it — grep shows only confirm_whole_corpus-style wiring), so correctness rests entirely on the caller flag. Severity is medium, not high: this is the documented intended design (docstring 108-115: "trusted caller obligation" because the engine receives "only an opaque statistic + p_value"), and exploitation requires a dishonest/buggy caller rather than a free post-hoc choice (the finding itself concedes it is smaller than G-001). It is nonetheless a genuine integrity gap — a pre-registered commitment (locked direction) is decoratively recorded but provides zero decision-time guarantee on this path, contradicting the framework's core premise that locks bind verdicts — and the suggested fix (engine derives supports = observed_relation == locked_direction from caller-supplied directional evidence) is implementable and would align the unit-level path with the whole-corpus guarantee.

b.why:
Independently verified against current code at /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py.

The finding is accurate. In confirm_unit_level (lines 136-179), the only use of the locked hypothesis.predicted_direction is a defense-in-depth SHAPE check (lines 162-168: rejects values not in {'greater','less'}). The verdict is then produced by verdict_from_pvalue (called at 171-179), which decides SUPPORTED vs CONTRADICTED solely on the trusted caller boolean direction_supports_claim (lines 129-132). The locked predicted_direction is NEVER cross-checked against the held-out statistic. The code itself documents this: lines 108-115 state statistic is recorded 'for provenance only (it does NOT gate the verdict)' and direction_supports_claim 'is a trusted caller obligation'.

PoC reproducible: a hypothesis with predicted_direction='greater', confirmed with direction_supports_claim=True, p_value<=alpha, powered=True returns Verdict.supported regardless of whether the statistic actually points 'greater' or 'less' relative to baseline. The locked 'greater' is never consulted at decision time.

Genuine concern (not false positive): predicted_direction is a real pre-registered/locked hypothesis field (hypothesis.py:73, part of lock content). Critically, there is an ASYMMETRY — the whole-corpus path (confirm_whole_corpus, lines 303-353) DOES enforce the locked direction at decision time via _resolve_direction → _empirical_p, computing support/contradict tails inside the engine. The unit-level path delegates that decision entirely to a free caller boolean. So a pre-registered field that governs the verdict on one path is decision-time-inert on the other, giving a false sense the locked direction binds the unit-level verdict when it does not. This is a real methodology-integrity gap surviving 3 hardening passes, and the suggested fix (derive supports from caller-supplied observed relation vs locked direction in-engine, mirroring whole-corpus) is sound.

Severity = medium (not high): exploitation requires a dishonest or buggy caller passing an inconsistent boolean, not free post-hoc tail selection (so strictly smaller than G-001), and the trust boundary is explicitly documented. confirm_unit_level has no internal engine caller — it is a study-facing primitive — so the risk is a study mis-wiring the flag, which the engine cannot detect today.

all.title: scrubbed_env() omits known off-box redirect env vars the Claude CLI/SDK honors (extra-CA / redirect vectors)
all.description: is_unsafe_subprocess_var() uses good shape rules for credentials/proxies (ANTHROPIC_*KEY/TOKEN, *_PROXY, AWS_*, GOOGLE_*) but the off-box-redirect set is a static allowlist of only three ANTHROPIC_*_BASE_URL names. Other documented transport-control variables the underlying node CLI / fetch stack honor are not covered — NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS and SSL_CERT_FILE (let an interceptor MITM egress with a rogue CA), and any future ANTHROPIC_* redirect that is neither a *_BASE_URL nor contains KEY/TOKEN (the docstring claims 'forward-proof' but the redirect half is a static list). Defense-in-depth: the loopback guard does not apply to the high-stakes off-box tier, so the env scrub is the only barrier for that subprocess.
all.poc: Set NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS=/tmp/rogue.pem (or a future ANTHROPIC_* redirect var) before a HIGH_STAKES call; scrubbed_env() leaves it intact, so the child trusts the attacker CA / honors the redirect.
all.suggested_fix: Make the off-box-redirect coverage shape-based to match the credential half: blank any ANTHROPIC_* and CLAUDE_CODE_* var except an explicit allowlist (CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN), and add NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS, SSL_CERT_FILE, SSL_CERT_DIR, REQUESTS_CA_BUNDLE, CURL_CA_BUNDLE to _UNSAFE_SUBPROCESS_VARS. Prefer an explicit allowlist for the subprocess env at this trust boundary.

a.why:
Confirmed in current code at src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py:136-163. is_unsafe_subprocess_var() scrubs metered ANTHROPIC_* creds (shape-based), a STATIC set of 7 redirect vars (lines 136-146), *_PROXY, AWS_*, GOOGLE_*. None of these rules match NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS, SSL_CERT_FILE, SSL_CERT_DIR, REQUESTS_CA_BUNDLE, or CURL_CA_BUNDLE — they don't start with ANTHROPIC_/AWS_/GOOGLE_, don't end with _PROXY, aren't in the set, and contain no KEY/TOKEN. So scrubbed_env() (lines 166-180) leaves them intact, and the SDK merge {**os.environ, **scrubbed} carries them into the child unchanged.

The strongest, load-bearing vector is NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS. The high-stakes tier executes the Claude CLI as a Node.js subprocess (explicitly documented at lines 9-11 and 515-517: "runs the Claude CLI subprocess (not the in-process Anthropic SDK)"). NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS is a standard Node TLS variable that appends a CA to the trust store process-wide; an attacker who controls the parent env makes the child trust a rogue CA and MITM the very egress ADR-0003 is meant to firewall. PoC reproduces: the var survives scrubbed_env() because it matches no rule.

This is a genuine defense-in-depth gap, not speculative or by-design: (a) the module's own threat model (#101 comment, line 134) explicitly targets vars that "redirect the subprocess OFF-BOX," and a rogue-CA MITM is exactly an off-box redirect of trust; (b) the loopback guard (require_loopback_url) is applied ONLY to the BULK tier (lines 336, 687), NOT to the high-stakes subprocess, so the env scrub is confirmed to be the sole barrier for this tier; (c) the docstring claims "forward-proof credential scrubbing" (lines 13-16) — true for the KEY/TOKEN shape half, but the redirect half is a static allowlist, so a future non-BASE_URL ANTHROPIC_* redirect would slip through, an asymmetry the finding correctly flags; (d) the current test matrix (tests/test_reasoning.py:51-71) does not include any CA-cert var, confirming the gap is untested.

The SSL_CERT_FILE/CA_BUNDLE additions are weaker (they affect Python/OpenSSL/curl, not necessarily the Node CLI path) but are reasonable hardening. Severity is medium rather than high because exploitation requires the attacker to already control the parent process environment (a meaningful precondition) and this is a defense-in-depth layer behind that; but within the stated no-metered-API / no-off-box-egress trust boundary, the gap is real and the suggested shape-based fix (blank ANTHROPIC_*/CLAUDE_CODE_* except CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN, plus add the CA-cert vars) is appropriate.

b.why:
Confirmed against current code in /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py (3 hardening passes applied). The finding is factually accurate and the PoC reproduces.

Facts verified:
- _UNSAFE_SUBPROCESS_VARS (seam.py:136-146) is a static frozenset of 7 names: ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL, ANTHROPIC_BEDROCK_BASE_URL, ANTHROPIC_VERTEX_BASE_URL, CLAUDE_CODE_USE_BEDROCK, CLAUDE_CODE_USE_VERTEX, CLAUDE_CODE_API_KEY_HELPER, GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS. It does NOT include NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS, SSL_CERT_FILE, SSL_CERT_DIR, REQUESTS_CA_BUNDLE, or CURL_CA_BUNDLE.
- is_unsafe_subprocess_var (seam.py:149-163) matches metered ANTHROPIC creds, the static set, *_PROXY, AWS_*, GOOGLE_*. NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS / SSL_CERT_* / *_CA_BUNDLE match none of these. I reproduced the exact logic: is_unsafe_subprocess_var returns False for all five CA-override vars.
- scrubbed_env (seam.py:166-180) therefore leaves NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS=/tmp/rogue.pem intact in the child env dict (PoC output: 'NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS in scrubbed child: /tmp/rogue.pem'). The SDK merge {**os.environ, **options.env} keeps it.

Why it is a genuine concern, not speculative/intended:
- The high-stakes tier (_high_stakes_complete, seam.py:394-421) spawns the claude CLI, a Node.js subprocess. Node's TLS stack honors NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS to add trusted root CAs; SSL_CERT_FILE/DIR are honored by OpenSSL-linked tooling. An attacker who controls the env before a HIGH_STAKES call injects a rogue CA and transparently MITMs the OAuth-authenticated egress — a strictly stronger redirect than ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL, which the code already scrubs. Defending ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL while leaving NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS open is an inconsistent trust boundary, so the gap is incomplete hardening of an explicit existing defense, not by-design.
- The off-box-redirect half is genuinely a static allowlist while the credential half is shape-based; the module docstring's 'forward-proof' claim (lines 13-16) does not extend to the redirect half. A future ANTHROPIC_* redirect that is neither *_BASE_URL nor KEY/TOKEN would also slip through.
- Defense-in-depth: require_loopback_url applies only to BULK, not HIGH_STAKES, so scrubbed_env() is the sole egress-integrity barrier for this tier (confirming the 'only barrier' claim).

Severity high (not critical): exploitation requires pre-existing control of the process environment (set the var before a HIGH_STAKES call) rather than being remotely triggerable. But env-var control (CI config, deploy units, shell profiles, a dependency that sets env) is a meaningfully lower bar than code execution and is exactly the class of attack the existing scrub already defends against. The suggested fix (shape-based ANTHROPIC_*/CLAUDE_CODE_* coverage minus the OAuth token, plus adding NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS/SSL_CERT_FILE/SSL_CERT_DIR/REQUESTS_CA_BUNDLE/CURL_CA_BUNDLE) is correct and a trivial extension of the existing pattern.

all.title: Gate.evaluate accesses decision.approved without confirming the precondition returned a GateDecision
all.description: Gate.evaluate calls self.precondition(context) (gates.py:87) then immediately reads decision.approved (:88) and decision.reason (:89). precondition is a Callable on the adapter trust boundary; a study that returns a bool/None/any non-GateDecision (easy mistake: returning True instead of GateDecision.approved(...)) triggers an opaque AttributeError ('bool' object has no attribute 'approved') rather than the module's typed GateError. Same contract-asymmetry class pass 3 closed for confirmer returns (#F-021) but left open for gate preconditions. The module guarantees 'a transition is legal only if the gate returns an approved decision' with GateError as the documented failure type.
all.poc: Gate(name='g', frm=..., to=..., precondition=lambda ctx: True).evaluate({}) -> AttributeError: 'bool' object has no attribute 'approved' (expected GateError).
all.suggested_fix: After calling: if not isinstance(decision, GateDecision): raise GateError(f"gate {self.name!r} precondition returned {type(decision).__name__}, expected GateDecision"). Optionally wrap the call to normalize a raising precondition into GateError.

a.why: Confirmed against current code (3 hardening passes applied). gates.py:87 calls decision = self.precondition(context); :88 immediately reads decision.approved with no isinstance(decision, GateDecision) guard. precondition is a Callable on the adapter/study trust boundary (gates.py:65), and the module documents GateError as THE failure type for a transition that is not legally approved (gates.py:6 "a transition is legal only if the gate returns an approved decision"; GateError docstring :37-38). A study returning True instead of GateDecision.approved(...) therefore raises an opaque AttributeError rather than the typed GateError. PoC reproduced exactly with the project's own interpreter (.venv/bin/python): Gate(precondition=lambda ctx: True).evaluate({}) -> AttributeError("'bool' object has no attribute 'approved'"). The asymmetry is real: pass 3 (#F-021) closed the identical class for the confirmer return at pipeline.py:560-567 (guards isinstance(verdict, Verdict), raises typed FirewallViolation) but the symmetric guard was never added to the gate precondition. Genuine, not speculative, not already-handled, not intended design — matches the project's established fix precedent.

b.why: Verified against current code. gates.py:87 calls self.precondition(context) and gates.py:88 immediately reads decision.approved with no type guard. precondition is a study-supplied Callable[[Mapping], GateDecision] on the adapter trust boundary (gates.py:65). A precondition that returns a non-GateDecision (e.g. lambda ctx: True) raises AttributeError: 'bool' object has no attribute 'approved' at line 88 rather than the module's documented GateError. The module docstring (gates.py:5-6) and GateError docstring (gates.py:37-38) guarantee GateError as the failure type for an illegal/non-approved transition. PoC is directly reproducible. The contract-asymmetry claim is also verified: pass 3 (#F-021) added the analogous isinstance guard for confirmer return values at pipeline.py:563 (and adjudication.py:255) precisely to convert this opaque-AttributeError class into a typed error, but the equivalent guard was not added for gate preconditions. This is the same defect class the team already chose to fix, left open here. Genuine robustness/production-readiness concern, not speculative and not intended design. Severity is low: it degrades a developer/integration error into a less-actionable exception type; it does not enable a silent gate bypass (the call still fails hard) and is not a security/data-integrity issue. Suggested fix (insert isinstance(decision, GateDecision) check raising GateError before line 88) is correct.

all.title: make_gate_node re-raises a recorder failure after the interrupt resume was already consumed (stranded-resume bricking)
all.description: In the gate node resume loop, once apply_gate_decision validates a resume value the node calls recorder(rec) (gatenode.py:145) inside the still-open while True loop, then returns. interrupt() (:137) has already consumed and persisted the operator's resume value into the checkpoint before validation. If recorder raises (trail clock raises, payload unserializable, provenance lock contended — all real ProvenanceError modes) the exception propagates out of node(). The module's own rationale (docstring 16-21, inline 133-135) is that a raise after the resume is consumed strands the value in the checkpoint and replays it forever, which is why a BAD resume re-fires interrupt instead of raising. A recorder failure on a GOOD resume hits exactly that hazard: the validated decision is lost, and on replay the node re-fires interrupt (already_decided False, recorder never wrote the terminal record), re-prompting the operator while the prior valid resume index is gone.
all.poc: def flaky_recorder(rec): raise RuntimeError('provenance trail unavailable'). make_gate_node(gate, propose=..., recorder=flaky_recorder); operator submits a valid 'approved' resume; recorder raises -> exception escapes node(); the approved resume was already persisted by interrupt() and is lost on replay.
all.suggested_fix: Make the in-loop recorder call resilient like the rest of the protocol: either attempt-record-then-return normalizing a recorder failure into a re-fired interrupt carrying the error, or record best-effort with a structured warning (mirroring pipeline.py:738-747 'never mask / never strand'). Do not let a recorder exception escape after interrupt() has consumed the resume value.

a.why:
CONFIRMED as a true production-readiness problem in current code.

Code matches the finding exactly. In /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/orchestration/gatenode.py, make_gate_node's node() runs a `while True` loop: line 137 `resumed = interrupt(current)`, line 139 validates via apply_gate_decision, then lines 143-145 call `recorder(rec)` with NO try/except, then line 146 returns. The recorder call sits after interrupt() has already consumed/persisted the operator's resume value for that interrupt index.

Technical premise is correct. LangGraph's interrupt/resume model re-executes the whole node from the top on resume; the supplied resume value is delivered to the matching interrupt and is tied to that graph step. If the node raises after interrupt() returns (recorder throwing), the node does not complete, its `{"gate_decisions": [...]}` write is never committed, and `already_decided` stays False. On the next replay the node re-fires interrupt() (line 137) and re-prompts — the prior validated decision is lost / the gate is stranded on the durable governance path. This is precisely the strand-and-replay hazard the module documents (docstring lines 15-20, inline 133-135) and deliberately guards against for BAD resumes (GateError -> re-fire interrupt, lines 140-142), but leaves unguarded for recorder failures on a GOOD resume.

The recorder-raises mode is real, not speculative. provenance.py raises ProvenanceError on clock failures (provenance.py:131), non-aware timestamps (138), serialization/normalization failures (143). Both gatenode.py:105 and graph.py:63-64 recommend the recorder be `lambda r: trail.record("gate", ...)`, so a ProvenanceError escaping recorder() is a realistic transient/contention mode.

Not already handled: lines 143-145 have no error handling, and the codebase's own established "never mask / never strand" convention (record-best-effort, structured warning, then proceed) is applied in src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:738-747 but NOT here — a self-evident inconsistency. No test covers a recorder that raises after a valid resume (tests in test_orchestration_graph.py cover bad-resume re-prompt and happy-path recording only), corroborating the gap.

Finding inaccuracy (minor, non-substantive): it cites "pipeline.py:738-747" implying orchestration/pipeline.py, which does not exist; the referenced never-mask/never-strand pattern actually lives in top-level src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:738-747. The pattern exists exactly as described, so this is only a path/citation imprecision.

Severity medium rather than high: genuine correctness/availability bug on the durable governance path, but (a) only triggers when a recorder is configured (the recommended secure config) AND the recorder actually raises, and (b) in the transient case it is operator-recoverable via re-submission; it becomes a hard gate-brick only if the recorder failure is persistent. No data corruption or security bypass. The suggested fix (normalize recorder failure into a re-fired interrupt carrying the error, or record best-effort with a structured warning mirroring pipeline.py:738-747) is appropriate.

b.why: Confirmed in current code. gatenode.py:136-146: interrupt() at :137 consumes and persists the operator resume into the checkpoint BEFORE validation. The bad-resume branch correctly re-fires interrupt (except GateError, :140-142) to avoid stranding, but on a GOOD resume recorder(rec) is called at :145 inside the still-open while-True loop with no guard. A recorder exception is NOT a GateError, so it propagates straight out of node(). Recorder raises are real, not speculative: ProvenanceTrail.record() / as_recorder()._record (provenance.py:116, 169-186, called by gatenode) raise ProvenanceError (a RuntimeError, not GateError) on clock failure (:130), non-datetime/non-tz clock (:133/138), unserializable/un-freezable payload (:143/156), or booby-trapped decision attributes (:176). When that escapes node(), the returned update was never applied to graph state and the recorder never wrote the terminal record, so on replay already_decided() (:123) is False and the node re-fires interrupt, re-prompting the operator while the previously-consumed valid approval is lost in the checkpoint — exactly the strand-and-brick hazard the module docstring (lines 15-20) and inline comment (133-135) exist to prevent. The asymmetry (re-fire on bad resume, raise on recorder failure of a good resume) violates the module's own never-strand invariant, and the codebase already has the resilient pattern to mirror (pipeline.py:738 'never mask the original failure'). PoC reproducible: recorder=lambda r: (_ for _ in ()).throw(RuntimeError('provenance trail unavailable')); a valid 'approved' resume causes the exception to escape node() after interrupt consumed the resume. Severity medium not high: gate is not permanently bricked (operator can re-submit on the re-prompt), trigger is an operational/infra recorder fault rather than routine attacker input, and failure mode is lost-approval + re-prompt rather than silent tamper. Not low because it directly breaks the stated safety invariant and can drop a recorded governance approval.

all.title: build_baseline_artifact lets hash_value raise raw ValueError/TypeError on non-finite or unhashable extra_inputs
all.description: At determinism.py:140 input_hashes[name] = hash_value(value) is called for every extra_input. hash_value -> canonical_json uses allow_nan=False and canonical_plain refuses non-JSON-native leaves, so a NaN/Inf float raises a bare ValueError ('Out of range float values are not JSON compliant') and an unhashable leaf raises a bare TypeError. preregistration.canonical_hypothesis_digest (preregistration.py:120-127) deliberately catches these and re-raises a typed, contextual PreRegistrationError naming the offending input; build_baseline_artifact — the other public consumer of the same canonical hashing — does not, so the determinism harness surfaces a low-context raw exception that does not identify which extra_input was bad.
all.poc: build_baseline_artifact(corpus, {}, extra_inputs={'cfg': {'thr': float('nan')}}) -> ValueError: Out of range float values are not JSON compliant (no indication extra_input 'cfg' is the culprit).
all.suggested_fix: Wrap per-input hashing: for name, value in (extra_inputs or {}).items(): try: input_hashes[name] = hash_value(value) except (ValueError, TypeError) as exc: raise ValueError(f"extra_input {name!r} cannot be canonicalized for a reproducible hash ({exc}); use JSON-native/finite values") from None.

a.why:
Verified against current code at /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay. build_baseline_artifact (determinism.py:99) loops over extra_inputs at lines 139-140 calling input_hashes[name] = hash_value(value) with no per-input try/except. hash_value -> canonical_json (_canonical.py:70) uses allow_nan=False, and canonical_plain (_canonical.py:39-65) refuses non-JSON-native leaves with a bare TypeError. I reproduced both cases by loading _canonical.py standalone: {'thr': float('nan')} -> "ValueError: Out of range float values are not JSON compliant: nan"; {'x': object()} -> "TypeError: cannot canonicalize a leaf of type builtins.'object' reproducibly". Neither message names the offending extra_input key. The asymmetry the finding cites is real: canonical_hypothesis_digest (preregistration.py:120-127) deliberately wraps the identical primitive in try/except (ValueError, TypeError) and re-raises a typed PreRegistrationError naming the hypothesis, while build_baseline_artifact does not. So the PoC is reproducible and the inconsistency is genuine.

However, the severity is low. The failure mode is a loud raise, not silent corruption or a wrong determinism record — the harness fails closed. The raw messages do carry content ("nan", the leaf type), so they are not opaque; the only missing detail is the extra_input *name*. This is a debuggability/consistency polish item, not a correctness, safety, or reproducibility defect. The suggested fix (wrap the per-input loop and name the key) is reasonable and matches the established preregistration pattern.

b.why: Verified against current code at /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay (3 passes applied). At src/assay_engine/baseline/determinism.py:140, build_baseline_artifact does `input_hashes[name] = hash_value(value)` per extra_input with no per-input try/except. hash_value re-exported from assay_engine._canonical: canonical_json (_canonical.py:69-71) calls json.dumps(..., allow_nan=False), so a NaN/Inf float raises bare ValueError; canonical_plain (_canonical.py:60-65) raises bare TypeError for any non-allowlisted leaf. PoC reproduced through the actual public entrypoint: build_baseline_artifact(corpus, {}, extra_inputs={'cfg': {'thr': float('nan')}}) -> ValueError: 'Out of range float values are not JSON compliant: nan' (no indication extra_input 'cfg' is the culprit); extra_inputs={'cfg': object()} -> bare TypeError. The sibling consumer of the same canonical primitive, canonical_hypothesis_digest at src/assay_engine/methodology/preregistration.py:119-127, deliberately wraps exactly these (except (ValueError, TypeError)) into a typed, contextual PreRegistrationError naming the hypothesis (per issue #82). build_baseline_artifact does not, so the determinism harness surfaces a low-context raw exception that does not identify the offending input — a real and project-acknowledged asymmetry. Note: the finding's path 'preregistration.py:120-127' is slightly off (file lives under methodology/, handler at lines 119-127), but the substance is accurate. Genuine concern, not speculative or already-handled.

all.title: Audit-gate tooling (pip-audit, pip-licenses) is installed unpinned, undermining the reproducibility the job enforces
all.description: The audit job pins uv to an exact version (ci.yml:103) for reproducibility and its whole purpose is a reproducible hash-pinned supply-chain scan, yet pip-audit is installed via python -m pip install --upgrade pip pip-audit (ci.yml:97, unpinned, no hashes) and pip-licenses via python -m pip install pip-licenses (:154, unpinned, confirmed). These tools run inside the trusted CI gate with checkout access; a malicious/broken release of pip-audit/pip-licenses (or a transitive dep) executes in CI and could suppress findings (a compromised pip-audit reporting clean) or break the gate — the same supply-chain risk class the job defends against, applied to the defender, and inconsistent with the uv pin.
all.poc: A compromised pip-audit patch release publishes; the next CI run pulls it via --upgrade and runs attacker-controlled code with the lockfile and CI token in scope; a tampered pip-audit could exit 0 on a vulnerable set, silently passing the gate.
all.suggested_fix: Pin pip-audit and pip-licenses to exact versions (matching the uv reproducibility posture), ideally from the hashed dev lockfile or with --require-hashes, adding them to the locked dev toolchain so they are covered by the same pinning/auditing.

a.why:
Verified directly against current code at /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/.github/workflows/ci.yml. Every factual claim holds:

- ci.yml:97 — `python -m pip install --upgrade pip pip-audit`: pip-audit is installed unpinned, with --upgrade (always latest), no version, no hashes. Confirmed.
- ci.yml:154 — `python -m pip install pip-licenses`: unpinned, no version, no hashes. Confirmed.
- ci.yml:103 — `python -m pip install uv==0.11.21`: uv IS pinned to an exact version explicitly for reproducibility (comment cites #F-031). Confirmed inconsistency.

When a study runs in DISCOVERY mode AND declares feature builders (the common case) the corpus units are iterated twice to construct the same set. Cheap to fix and trivially correct.

all.suggested_fix: Hoist a single corpus_unit_ids = {u.unit_id for u in corpus.units} once before the DISCOVERY split check and reuse it for both the split-drift guard (436-441) and the feature-builder ghost-id guard (454-459); drop the duplicate comprehension.

all.title: Comment claims the determinism freeze keeps an array 'opaque/O(1)' but freeze() copies the whole buffer via tobytes() (O(N))
all.description: The docstring of cosine_similarity_matrix_array() (primitives.py:125) states 'the determinism freeze keeps an array opaque/O(1), unlike a nested list'. The actual freeze() path for an array-like (_frozen.py ~109-118) calls value.tobytes() and wraps it in a (kind, shape, bytes) descriptor, re-freezing that. tobytes() materializes/copies every byte — O(N) in elements, not O(1). The intended contrast is real (one tobytes() call rather than per-element Python recursion) but the literal 'O(1)' claim is false; both memory and time for freezing an array are O(N).
all.poc: For an ndarray of n elements freeze() executes tobytes(), allocating/copying n*itemsize bytes — linear in n; a 10M-element array produces a 10M*itemsize-byte bytes object each freeze. 'O(1)' is therefore false.
all.suggested_fix: Soften the comment to what is true: the freeze traverses an array in a single tobytes() pass (O(1) traversal depth / no per-element Python recursion) but the conversion is O(N) in array size. Replace 'opaque/O(1)' with 'opaque, frozen in a single tobytes() pass (no per-element recursion), unlike a nested list'.

a.why: Verified against current code. primitives.py:125 docstring of cosine_similarity_matrix_array() states "the determinism freeze keeps an array opaque/O(1), unlike a nested list". The freeze path is in src/assay_engine/_frozen.py (not baseline/_frozen.py as the finding's path hint said, but the code is present): lines 109-118 detect an array-like with tobytes() and at line 118 return freeze((str(kind), tuple(shape)..., tobytes())). value.tobytes() materializes/copies the entire buffer — O(N) in element count and itemsize for both time and memory. The PoC is reproducible: a 10M-element ndarray yields a 10M*itemsize-byte bytes object on every freeze. The literal "O(1)" claim is therefore false. The intended contrast (a single tobytes() pass with no per-element Python recursion, vs nested-list per-element freeze recursion) is real, but the "O(1)" wording is wrong. Confirmed as a true documentation-accuracy defect in current code. Severity is low: it is solely a misleading docstring comment; the runtime behavior is correct, there is no functional/correctness/security/performance regression introduced by the comment, and the array path is not actually claimed to be unused at scale incorrectly. It is a genuine but minor concern, well addressed by the suggested softening of the wording.

b.why: Verified in current code. The docstring at /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/baseline/primitives.py:125 states "the determinism freeze keeps an array opaque/O(1), unlike a nested list". The freeze path is at /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/_frozen.py:109-118 (note: the finding cited _frozen.py under baseline/, but the actual file is at src/assay_engine/_frozen.py). At line 118 freeze() canonicalizes an array-like by calling tobytes(): `return freeze((str(kind), tuple(shape)..., tobytes()))`. tobytes() materializes/copies the entire buffer (n*itemsize bytes), which is O(N) in both time and memory, not O(1). The PoC is reproducible: a 10M-element ndarray yields a 10M*itemsize-byte bytes object on each freeze. The literal "O(1)" claim is therefore false. The intended contrast (single tobytes pass vs per-element Python recursion for a nested list) is real and is the genuine engineering benefit, but the asymptotic claim as written is incorrect. This is a documentation-accuracy defect only: no runtime, correctness, security, or data-integrity impact — the freeze produces a correct, hashable, deterministic descriptor regardless. Severity is low (misleading comment that could lead a builder to wrongly assume freezing a large array is free).
